docs.title: squirrel
docs.text: Database-aware backup for PostgreSQL and MySQL/MariaDB. Content-addressed, AES-256-GCM encrypted, and deduplicated — with first-class support for WAL streaming, binlog capture, and physical base backups.

docs.title: Features
docs.text:
• PostgreSQL

    Physical base backup via the replication protocol + continuous WAL streaming. PITR to any point in time. No pg_dump, no superuser.

    PostgreSQL docs

  • MySQL / MariaDB

    Logical SQL dump + binlog streaming (position and GTID). Physical data-dir backup. Restore to any binlog position.

    MySQL docs

  • Encryption

    AES-256-GCM per blob with a random nonce. Argon2id key derivation. Multi-password key management. Zero plaintext on the backend.

    Encryption

  • Deduplication

    Rabin CDC variable-size chunking (512 KiB – 8 MiB). SHA-256 content addressing. Dedup across hosts, time, and database types.

    Deduplication

  • Storage Backends

    Local filesystem, S3/MinIO, Azure Blob Storage, Google Cloud Storage, SFTP. Pluggable — one config, any backend.

    Backends

  • Secret Providers

    env, file, OS keyring, HashiCorp Vault, Mozilla SOPS, age, 1Password, arbitrary shell commands. Secrets never in plaintext config.

    Secret Providers

  • Scheduling & Daemon

    Built-in cron daemon with Prometheus metrics. Native OS integration via systemd, launchd, and Windows Task Scheduler.

    Scheduling

  • FUSE Mount

    Mount any snapshot as a read-only filesystem. Browse and copy individual files without a full restore.

    mount command

docs.title: Commands
docs.text:
• Repository

    init · check · stats · prune · key

  • Backup

    backup · backup postgres · backup mysql

  • Restore

    restore · restore postgres · restore mysql

  • Snapshots

    snapshots · forget · diff · mount

  • Configuration

    config · run · secrets

  • Daemon

    daemon · schedule · pg

docs.title: Quick Start
docs.text:
# Initialize a repository
squirrel init --repo /backup/myrepo

# Back up a directory
squirrel backup /home/user --repo /backup/myrepo

# Back up PostgreSQL (base + WAL)
squirrel backup postgres \
  --dsn "postgres://squirrel:secret@localhost/postgres" \
  --repo /backup/pg-repo --slot squirrel

# List snapshots
squirrel snapshots --repo /backup/myrepo

# Restore a snapshot
squirrel restore abc12345 --repo /backup/myrepo --target /restore/dir

Full Quickstart

docs.title: Architecture
docs.text:
flowchart LR
    SRC([files / PostgreSQL / MySQL]) --> CDC[Rabin CDC\nchunking]
    CDC --> SHA[SHA-256\nblob ID]
    SHA --> DEDUP{in repo?}
    DEDUP -- yes --> SKIP([skip])
    DEDUP -- no --> ZSTD[zstd\ncompression]
    ZSTD --> AES[AES-256-GCM\nencryption]
    AES --> PACK[pack file]
    PACK --> BE([local / S3 / GCS / Azure / SFTP])

    style SKIP fill:#1a1a1a,stroke:#f5a623,color:#f5a623

Architecture overview

docs.title: Binary Releases
docs.text:
Pre-built binaries for Linux, macOS, and Windows (amd64 / arm64) are available on the GitHub Releases page.

Download the archive for your platform, extract the binary, and place it somewhere on your PATH.

docs.title: Homebrew (macOS / Linux)
docs.text: brew install elpol4k0/tap/squirrel

docs.title: Go
docs.text:
Requires Go 1.21 or later.

go install github.com/elpol4k0/squirrel/cmd/squirrel@latest

docs.title: Docker
docs.text:
docker pull ghcr.io/elpol4k0/squirrel:latest

Run a backup inside Docker:

docker run --rm \
  -e SQUIRREL_PASSWORD=secret \
  -v /data:/data:ro \
  -v /backup:/backup \
  ghcr.io/elpol4k0/squirrel:latest \
  backup --path /data --repo /backup/repo

docs.title: Without WinFsp (all commands except mount)
docs.text:
$env:CGO_ENABLED = "0"
go build -o squirrel.exe .\cmd\squirrel\

Note

CGO_ENABLED=0 disables the mount command, which requires the WinFsp SDK. All other commands work fully without it.

docs.title: With WinFsp (enables mount)
docs.text:
1. Install WinFsp from the official website.
  2. Build without disabling CGO:
go build -o squirrel.exe .\cmd\squirrel\

docs.title: Shell Completions
docs.text:
After installation, enable shell completions for a better CLI experience:

BashZshFishPowerShell 
squirrel completion bash > /etc/bash_completion.d/squirrel

squirrel completion zsh > "${fpath[1]}/_squirrel"

squirrel completion fish > ~/.config/fish/completions/squirrel.fish

squirrel completion powershell | Out-String | Invoke-Expression

See squirrel completion for details.

docs.title: Verifying the Installation
docs.text:
squirrel --version

Expected output: squirrel v1.x.x

docs.title: Quickstart
docs.text: This page walks through the most common workflows from scratch.

docs.title: 1. Initialize a Repository
docs.text:
squirrel init --repo /backup/myrepo
# Enter a password when prompted

Expected output: 

Repository initialised at /backup/myrepo

docs.title: 2. Back Up a Directory
docs.text:
squirrel backup --repo /backup/myrepo --path /home/user/data --tag daily

Expected output: 

Snapshot abc12345 saved  (42 blobs, 128 MiB)

docs.title: 3. List Snapshots
docs.text: squirrel snapshots --repo /backup/myrepo

docs.title: 4. Restore a Snapshot
docs.text: squirrel restore abc12345 --repo /backup/myrepo --target /restore/data

docs.title: 5. Apply Retention Policy
docs.text: squirrel forget --repo /backup/myrepo --keep-last 7 --keep-daily 30 --prune

docs.title: Requirements
docs.text:
• PostgreSQL 12+
  • A replication user with REPLICATION privilege
  • wal_level = replica (or logical)

docs.title: Base Backup + WAL Streaming
docs.text:
squirrel backup postgres \
  --dsn "postgres://replicator:pw@localhost/postgres?sslmode=disable" \
  --repo s3:mybucket/pg-backup \
  --slot squirrel \
  --tag production

Press Ctrl-C to stop WAL streaming once enough WAL has been captured.

docs.title: WAL-Only (after a base backup exists)
docs.text:
squirrel backup postgres \
  --dsn "postgres://replicator:pw@localhost/postgres?sslmode=disable" \
  --repo s3:mybucket/pg-backup \
  --slot squirrel \
  --wal-only

docs.title: Restore with PITR
docs.text:
squirrel restore postgres abc12345 \
  --repo s3:mybucket/pg-backup \
  --target /var/lib/postgresql/17/main \
  --pitr "2026-04-20 14:30:00" \
  --verify

docs.title: Requirements
docs.text:
• MySQL 5.7 / 8.x or MariaDB 10.5+
  • Binary logging enabled: log_bin = ON
  • User privileges: RELOAD, REPLICATION SLAVE, REPLICATION CLIENT, SELECT

docs.title: Logical Dump + Binlog Streaming
docs.text:
squirrel backup mysql \
  --dsn "root:pw@tcp(localhost:3306)/" \
  --repo /backup/mysql \
  --database myapp \
  --tag daily

docs.title: Restore SQL Dump
docs.text:
squirrel restore mysql abc12345 \
  --repo /backup/mysql \
  --dsn "root:pw@tcp(localhost:3306)/" \
  --sql-only

docs.title: Extract Binlogs for PITR
docs.text:
squirrel restore mysql abc12345 \
  --repo /backup/mysql \
  --dsn "root:pw@tcp(localhost:3306)/" \
  --binlog-dir /tmp/binlogs

Replay with mysqlbinlog:

mysqlbinlog /tmp/binlogs/*.bin | mysql -uroot -p

docs.title: S3 Backend
docs.text:
export AWS_ACCESS_KEY_ID=...
export AWS_SECRET_ACCESS_KEY=...

squirrel init --repo s3:mybucket/prefix
squirrel backup --path /data --repo s3:mybucket/prefix

docs.title: Using a Config File
docs.text:
Create ~/.config/squirrel/config.yml (Linux/macOS) or %APPDATA%\squirrel\config.yml (Windows):

version: 1

repositories:
  production:
    url: s3:mybucket/prod
    password: ${env:SQUIRREL_PASSWORD}

profiles:
  pg-daily:
    repository: production
    type: postgres
    dsn: ${env:PG_DSN}
    slot: squirrel
    schedule: "0 2 * * *"
    retention:
      keep-daily: 7
      keep-weekly: 4
      keep-monthly: 12
      prune: true
    hooks:
      post-success:
        - webhook: https://monitoring.example.com/healthcheck/pg-daily

Run the profile:

squirrel run pg-daily

See Configuration for the complete reference.

docs.title: Architecture
docs.text: squirrel is a content-addressed, encrypted backup system. This page explains the key architectural decisions and how data flows from source to storage.

docs.title: Data Flow
docs.text:
flowchart TD
    A([Source data\nfiles / PostgreSQL / MySQL]) --> B[Rabin CDC\n512 KiB - 8 MiB chunks]
    B --> C[SHA-256\ncontent-addressed blob ID]
    C --> D{Already in repo?}
    D -- Yes --> SKIP([skip - deduplication])
    D -- No --> E[zstd lvl 3\ncompression]
    E --> F[AES-256-GCM\nrandom nonce per blob]
    F --> G[Pack file\ngroup blobs for upload]
    G --> H([Backend storage\nlocal / S3 / GCS / Azure / SFTP])

    style SKIP fill:#1a1a1a,stroke:#f5a623,color:#f5a623
    style A fill:#1a1a1a,stroke:#555
    style H fill:#1a1a1a,stroke:#555

docs.title: Component Overview
docs.text:
flowchart LR
    subgraph CLI["squirrel CLI"]
        B[backup] & R[restore] & S[snapshots] & F[forget] & P[prune] & C[check] & RN[run]
    end

    CLI --> REPO

    subgraph REPO["Repo Layer"]
        IDX[blob index] & SNAP[snapshots / trees] & GC[prune / GC]
    end

    REPO --> CRYPTO

    subgraph CRYPTO["Packer / Crypto"]
        CDC[Rabin CDC] --> ZSTD[zstd] --> AES[AES-256-GCM]
    end

    CRYPTO --> BACKENDS

    subgraph BACKENDS["Storage Backends"]
        L[local] & S3[S3 / MinIO] & GCS[GCS] & AZ[Azure] & SFTP[SFTP]
    end

docs.title: Repository Layout
docs.text:
repo/
├── config           # Encrypted repo metadata (compression level, version)
├── keys/            # Password-wrapped master key copies
│   ├── <key-id-1>
│   └── <key-id-2>
├── data/            # Pack files (2-character hex prefix sharding)
│   ├── ab/
│   │   ├── ab1234...pack
│   │   └── ab5678...pack
│   └── cd/
│       └── cd9012...pack
├── index/           # Blob ID → (pack file, offset, length) mappings
│   ├── index-1
│   └── index-2
├── snapshots/       # Snapshot manifests (JSON, encrypted)
│   ├── snapshot-abc12345
│   └── snapshot-b2c3d4e5
└── locks/           # Concurrency advisory locks

docs.title: Pack File Format
docs.text:
flowchart LR
    B0["blob_0\nencrypted"] --- B1["blob_1\nencrypted"] --- DOTS[" ... "] --- HDR["encrypted header\nblob ID to offset"] --- LEN["header_len\n4 bytes LE"]

    style B0 fill:#1a1a1a,stroke:#f5a623
    style B1 fill:#1a1a1a,stroke:#f5a623
    style HDR fill:#1a1a1a,stroke:#555
    style LEN fill:#1a1a1a,stroke:#555
    style DOTS fill:#0d0d0d,stroke:#333

Each encrypted region uses the format:

nonce(12 bytes) || AES-256-GCM ciphertext || GCM-tag(16 bytes)

Read sequence to locate a blob:

sequenceDiagram
    autonumber
    participant C as Client
    participant P as Pack file
    C->>P: Read last 4 bytes to get header_len
    C->>P: Seek to file_size - 4 - header_len
    C->>P: Decrypt header, get blob ID map
    C->>P: Seek to blob offset and decrypt blob

docs.title: Snapshot Structure
docs.text:
erDiagram
    SNAPSHOT {
        string id
        string time
        string hostname
        string type
        string tree
    }
    TREE {
        string nodes
    }
    FILE_NODE {
        string name
        string type
        int mode
        string mtime
        string content
    }
    DIR_NODE {
        string name
        string subtree
    }
    BLOB {
        string sha256_id
        bytes data
    }

    SNAPSHOT ||--|| TREE : "tree"
    TREE ||--o{ FILE_NODE : contains
    TREE ||--o{ DIR_NODE : contains
    DIR_NODE ||--|| TREE : "subtree"
    FILE_NODE ||--o{ BLOB : "content"

docs.title: Concurrency Model
docs.text:
stateDiagram-v2
    [*] --> Idle
    Idle --> Locked : backup / prune / check
    Idle --> Reading : snapshots / restore
    Locked --> Idle : operation complete
    Reading --> Idle : done
    Locked --> Locked : concurrent lock attempt

docs.title: Further Reading
docs.text:
• Encryption — Key management, AES-GCM, Argon2id
  • Deduplication — Rabin CDC, SHA-256, content addressing

docs.title: Deduplication
docs.text: squirrel uses content-addressed storage to automatically deduplicate identical data across files, snapshots, hosts, and time. Only unique chunks are ever stored.

docs.title: Content Addressing
docs.text:
Every blob is identified by the SHA-256 hash of its plaintext (before compression and encryption). This means:

  • The same file backed up twice produces the same blob ID — the second backup writes nothing.
  • The same content on two different machines deduplicates across hosts.
  • Modified files produce different chunk IDs only for the changed chunks.

docs.title: Rabin CDC Chunking
docs.text: squirrel uses Rabin fingerprint Content-Defined Chunking (CDC) to split data into variable-size chunks. Unlike fixed-size chunking, CDC is shift-resistant: inserting bytes at the beginning of a file does not invalidate all subsequent chunks.

docs.title: Parameters
docs.text: Parameter Value Algorithm Rabin fingerprint (polynomial 0x3DA3358B4DC173) Minimum chunk size 512 KiB Maximum chunk size 8 MiB Average chunk size ~4 MiB

docs.title: How CDC Works
docs.text:
flowchart LR
    DATA([byte stream]) --> WIN[sliding\nhash window]
    WIN --> HASH{Rabin hash\nmatches target?}
    HASH -- No --> WIN
    HASH -- Yes --> CUT[cut chunk\nboundary]
    CUT --> ID[SHA-256\nblob ID]
    ID --> DUP{exists in\nindex?}
    DUP -- Yes --> SKIP([skip upload])
    DUP -- No --> UPLOAD([compress then encrypt then upload])

    style SKIP fill:#1a1a1a,stroke:#f5a623,color:#f5a623
    style UPLOAD fill:#1a1a1a,stroke:#555

docs.title: Shift-Resistance
docs.text: Scenario Fixed-size chunks Rabin CDC Insert 1 byte at start of file All chunks shift — 0% dedup Only ~2 boundary chunks change Insert text in middle of large file All subsequent chunks change ~2 boundary chunks change Two identical files 100% dedup 100% dedup Two files differing by 1 byte ~1 chunk deduplicated Most chunks deduplicated

docs.title: Deduplication Scope
docs.text:
flowchart LR
    subgraph Sources
        H1[host A\ndaily backup]
        H2[host B\ndaily backup]
        PG[PostgreSQL\nbase backup]
        MY[MySQL\nphysical backup]
    end

    subgraph Repo["Repository (shared blob pool)"]
        BLOB1[blob aaaa...]
        BLOB2[blob bbbb...]
        BLOB3[blob cccc...]
    end

    H1 & H2 & PG & MY --> Repo

Deduplication works across time, hosts, database types, and snapshot types — all sharing the same blob pool.

docs.title: In-Session Deduplication
docs.text: During a backup session, squirrel maintains a pending map of blob IDs being uploaded in the current session. A blob is not uploaded twice even if the persistent index has not been flushed yet.

docs.title: Persistent Index
docs.text:
The persistent index in index/ maps blob IDs to pack file locations:

blob-sha256 → (pack-file-id, byte-offset, length)

Before uploading any chunk, squirrel checks both the in-memory pending map and the persistent index. If the blob ID is found in either, the upload is skipped entirely.

docs.title: Pack File Grouping
docs.text:
flowchart LR
    B1[blob] & B2[blob] & B3[blob] --> PACK[pack file\n~128 MiB target]
    PACK --> UPLOAD([single upload\nto backend])

Grouping blobs into pack files reduces:

  • S3 PUT request count (and cost)
  • SFTP file count overhead
  • Index size

Blobs within a pack file are stored contiguously; the pack header maps blob ID → offset for random access during restore.

docs.title: Measuring Deduplication
docs.text:
squirrel stats --repo /backup/myrepo --dedup

Storage size:  4.2 GiB
Logical size:  18.7 GiB
Dedup ratio:   4.45x

Dedup ratio = Logical size / Storage size

A ratio of 4.45× means the repository stores 18.7 GiB of data in only 4.2 GiB — a 76% reduction.

docs.title: Encryption
docs.text: squirrel encrypts all data at rest using AES-256-GCM with per-blob random nonces. The master key is derived from a password using Argon2id.

docs.title: Generation
docs.text:
At squirrel init, a 256-bit (32-byte) random master key is generated using a cryptographically secure random source. This master key is used to encrypt all blobs and metadata.

The master key itself is never stored in plaintext — only password-wrapped copies are stored in keys/.

docs.title: Key Wrapping
docs.text:
flowchart TD
    PW([password + salt]) --> KDF["Argon2id\ntime=3, memory=64 MiB, threads=4"]
    KDF --> KEK[key-encryption key]
    KEK --> WRAP["AES-256-GCM\nwrap master key with KEK"]
    WRAP --> STORE([stored in keys/key-id])

docs.title: Adding Passwords
docs.text:
Each squirrel key add operation:

  1. Decrypts the master key using the existing password.
  2. Generates a new random salt.
  3. Derives a new KEK from the new password + salt via Argon2id.
  4. Wraps the master key with the new KEK.
  5. Stores the new wrapped key in keys/.

No blob re-encryption is needed — the master key does not change.

docs.title: Password Rotation
docs.text:
sequenceDiagram
    autonumber
    participant U as User
    participant SQ as squirrel
    participant KD as keys dir
    U->>SQ: squirrel key add
    SQ->>KD: decrypt existing wrapped key
    SQ->>SQ: generate new salt, derive new KEK
    SQ->>KD: write new wrapped key
    U->>SQ: squirrel key remove old-id
    SQ->>KD: delete old wrapped key

docs.title: Per-Blob Encryption
docs.text:
Every blob (file chunk, tree, snapshot, index) is individually encrypted before storage:

flowchart TD
    PT([plaintext blob]) --> ZSTD["zstd level 3\ncompression"]
    ZSTD --> NONCE["generate random\n96-bit nonce"]
    NONCE --> GCM["AES-256-GCM\nencrypt with master key"]
    GCM --> OUT(["nonce || ciphertext || GCM-tag\n12 B + N B + 16 B"])

docs.title: AES-256-GCM Properties
docs.text:
Property Value Algorithm AES-256-GCM Key size 256 bits (32 bytes) Nonce size 96 bits (12 bytes) Authentication tag 128 bits (16 bytes) Nonce per blob Randomly generated

The GCM authentication tag provides integrity protection — any tampering with the ciphertext is detected during decryption.

docs.title: Key ID
docs.text: The key ID stored in keys/ is the SHA-256 hash of the wrapped key material. It is used to select the correct key when opening the repository with a given password.

docs.title: Password Verification
docs.text:
flowchart TD
    START([open repository]) --> LIST[list all keys]
    LIST --> LOOP{for each key}
    LOOP --> DERIVE["derive KEK from\npassword + key salt"]
    DERIVE --> TRY["attempt AES-256-GCM\ndecrypt wrapped key"]
    TRY -- tag OK --> SUCCESS([master key recovered])
    TRY -- tag fail --> LOOP
    LOOP -- no keys left --> FAIL([authentication failed])

docs.title: Security Properties
docs.text: Property Details Confidentiality AES-256-GCM — no plaintext stored on backend Integrity GCM authentication tag per blob Password-based KDF Argon2id — memory-hard, resistant to brute-force Nonce uniqueness Randomly generated per blob — nonce reuse is statistically negligible Forward secrecy Not provided — compromising the master key exposes all data Multi-password Multiple KEKs wrap the same master key independently

docs.title: Changing the Repository Password
docs.text:
# Add new password
squirrel key add --repo /backup/myrepo

# Verify new password works
squirrel snapshots --repo /backup/myrepo

# Remove old password
squirrel key list --repo /backup/myrepo
squirrel key remove <old-key-id> --repo /backup/myrepo

docs.title: Storage Backends
docs.text: squirrel supports multiple storage backends. The backend is selected by the URL scheme in the url field of a repository definition.

docs.title: Supported Backends
docs.text:
• Local Filesystem

    Local or NFS-mounted directory. Zero dependencies, fastest for single-machine setups.

    URL: /path/to/repo or file:///path/to/repo

    Local docs

  • S3 / MinIO

    Amazon S3 or any S3-compatible backend (MinIO, Wasabi, Backblaze B2). Multi-region, lifecycle policies.

    URL: s3:bucket/prefix

    S3 docs

  • Google Cloud Storage

    Google Cloud object storage. Authenticated via ADC or service account JSON.

    URL: gs:bucket/prefix

    GCS docs

  • Azure Blob Storage

    Microsoft Azure Blob storage. Authenticated via connection string or managed identity.

    URL: az:container/prefix

    Azure docs

  • SFTP

    SSH File Transfer Protocol. Works with any SSH server. Ideal for off-site backup over SSH.

    URL: sftp://user@host/path

    SFTP docs

docs.title: Choosing a Backend
docs.text: Scenario Recommended Single machine, local disk Local filesystem Cloud-native, scalable S3, GCS, or Azure Self-hosted object storage MinIO (S3-compatible) Off-site via SSH SFTP Air-gapped / no internet Local filesystem or SFTP

docs.title: Repository Layout
docs.text:
All backends store the same directory structure:

<repo>/
├── config           # Encrypted repository configuration
├── keys/            # Password-wrapped master keys
├── data/            # Pack files (2-char hex prefix sharding)
│   ├── ab/
│   │   ├── ab1234...pack
│   │   └── ab5678...pack
│   └── cd/
├── index/           # Blob ID → pack location mappings
├── snapshots/       # Snapshot manifests (encrypted JSON)
└── locks/           # Concurrency advisory locks

docs.title: Pack File Format
docs.text:
Every backend stores the same internal format — pack files containing groups of AES-256-GCM encrypted blobs:

[blob_0][blob_1]…[blob_N][encrypted_header][header_len: 4 bytes LE]

Each encrypted region: nonce(12 bytes) || ciphertext || GCM-tag(16 bytes)

The header maps blob IDs → byte offsets within the pack file.

docs.title: Multi-Repo Strategy
docs.text:
Back up to multiple repositories per profile using extends::

profiles:
  pg-prod:
    repository: s3-prod
    type: postgres
    dsn: ${vault-prod:secret/postgres#dsn}
    slot: squirrel

  pg-prod-offsite:
    extends: pg-prod
    repository: sftp-offsite
    schedule: "0 5 * * 0"   # weekly offsite copy

docs.title: Azure Blob Storage
docs.text: Store repository data in a Microsoft Azure Blob Storage container.

docs.title: URL Format
docs.text:
az:container/prefix
az:account/container/prefix

docs.title: Configuration
docs.text:
repositories:
  azure:
    url: az:mycontainer/squirrel
    password: ${env:SQUIRREL_PASSWORD}
    env:
      AZURE_STORAGE_ACCOUNT: mystorageaccount
      AZURE_STORAGE_KEY: ${env:AZURE_STORAGE_KEY}

docs.title: Storage Account Key (recommended for automation)
docs.text:
repositories:
  azure:
    url: az:mycontainer/squirrel
    password: ${vault-prod:secret/squirrel#azure_repo_password}
    env:
      AZURE_STORAGE_ACCOUNT: mystorageaccount
      AZURE_STORAGE_KEY: ${env:AZURE_STORAGE_KEY}

Retrieve the key from the Azure Portal under Storage Account → Access Keys.

docs.title: Connection String
docs.text:
repositories:
  azure:
    url: az:mycontainer/squirrel
    password: ${env:SQUIRREL_PASSWORD}
    env:
      AZURE_STORAGE_CONNECTION_STRING: ${env:AZURE_STORAGE_CONNECTION_STRING}

docs.title: Managed Identity (Azure VMs / AKS)
docs.text: When running on an Azure VM or AKS pod with a Managed Identity, credentials are resolved automatically from the instance metadata service. No key or connection string is needed.

docs.title: Environment Variables
docs.text: Variable Description AZURE_STORAGE_ACCOUNT Storage account name AZURE_STORAGE_KEY Storage account access key AZURE_STORAGE_CONNECTION_STRING Full connection string (alternative to account + key)

docs.title: Notes
docs.text:
• The container must exist before running squirrel init.
  • Enable Blob soft delete and Container soft delete in the storage account for extra protection.
  • Azure Blob Storage supports immutable storage (WORM policies) for compliance requirements.
  • squirrel uses Block Blobs with append semantics for pack files.

docs.title: Google Cloud Storage
docs.text: Store repository data in a Google Cloud Storage bucket.

docs.title: URL Format
docs.text: gs:bucket/prefix

docs.title: Configuration
docs.text:
repositories:
  gcs:
    url: gs:my-gcs-bucket/squirrel
    password: ${env:SQUIRREL_PASSWORD}
    env:
      GOOGLE_APPLICATION_CREDENTIALS: /etc/squirrel/gcs-sa.json

docs.title: Authentication
docs.text:
GCS uses Application Default Credentials (ADC). Credentials are resolved in this order:

  1. GOOGLE_APPLICATION_CREDENTIALS — Path to a service account JSON key file.
  2. gcloud CLI — Credentials from gcloud auth application-default login.
  3. Metadata server — Automatically used on GCE/GKE/Cloud Run.

docs.title: Service Account Key File
docs.text:
repositories:
  gcs:
    url: gs:my-gcs-bucket/squirrel
    password: ${env:SQUIRREL_PASSWORD}
    env:
      GOOGLE_APPLICATION_CREDENTIALS: /etc/squirrel/gcs-sa.json

Create a service account with Storage Object Admin on the bucket:

gcloud iam service-accounts create squirrel-backup \
  --display-name "squirrel backup"

gsutil iam ch \
  serviceAccount:squirrel-backup@PROJECT.iam.gserviceaccount.com:roles/storage.objectAdmin \
  gs://my-gcs-bucket

gcloud iam service-accounts keys create /etc/squirrel/gcs-sa.json \
  --iam-account squirrel-backup@PROJECT.iam.gserviceaccount.com

docs.title: Workload Identity (GKE)
docs.text: When running on GKE with Workload Identity configured, no GOOGLE_APPLICATION_CREDENTIALS is needed — the metadata server provides credentials automatically.

docs.title: Notes
docs.text:
• The bucket must exist before running squirrel init.
  • Enable Uniform bucket-level access on the bucket for simpler IAM management.
  • GCS Object Versioning can be enabled for extra protection.
  • squirrel uploads blobs using standard GCS Object API (no multipart for small blobs, resumable uploads for large pack files).

docs.title: Local Filesystem
docs.text: Store repository data in a local directory or any mounted filesystem (NFS, SMB, external drive).

docs.title: URL Format
docs.text:
/absolute/path/to/repo
file:///absolute/path/to/repo

docs.title: Configuration
docs.text:
repositories:
  local:
    url: /backup/squirrel-repo
    password: ${env:SQUIRREL_PASSWORD}

No additional environment variables are required.

docs.title: Standard local backup
docs.text:
repositories:
  local:
    url: /backup/myrepo
    password: ${env:SQUIRREL_PASSWORD}

docs.title: External drive
docs.text:
repositories:
  external:
    url: /mnt/backup-drive/squirrel
    password: ${file:/etc/squirrel/repo.key}

docs.title: Windows
docs.text:
repositories:
  local:
    url: D:\Backup\squirrel-repo
    password: ${env:SQUIRREL_PASSWORD}

docs.title: Atomicity
docs.text: The local backend writes blobs to a temporary file first, then renames it atomically into the final location. This prevents partial writes from corrupting the repository in case of a crash.

docs.title: Notes
docs.text:
• The directory is created automatically on squirrel init if it does not exist.
  • On NFS mounts, ensure the mount supports atomic rename operations.
  • Concurrent access from multiple hosts is supported via advisory locks in locks/.
  • For network-attached storage with poor NFS semantics, prefer SFTP or an object storage backend.

docs.title: S3 / MinIO
docs.text: Store repository data in any S3-compatible object storage service: Amazon S3, MinIO, Backblaze B2, Wasabi, Cloudflare R2, or any other S3-compatible endpoint.

docs.title: URL Format
docs.text:
s3:bucket/prefix
s3:region/bucket/prefix

docs.title: Configuration
docs.text:
repositories:
  s3-prod:
    url: s3:my-bucket/squirrel/prod
    password: ${env:SQUIRREL_PASSWORD}
    env:
      AWS_ACCESS_KEY_ID: ${env:AWS_ACCESS_KEY_ID}
      AWS_SECRET_ACCESS_KEY: ${env:AWS_SECRET_ACCESS_KEY}
      AWS_DEFAULT_REGION: eu-central-1

docs.title: Environment Variables
docs.text:
Variable Description AWS_ACCESS_KEY_ID S3 access key ID AWS_SECRET_ACCESS_KEY S3 secret access key AWS_DEFAULT_REGION AWS region (default: us-east-1) AWS_ENDPOINT_URL Custom endpoint for MinIO or S3-compatible services AWS_SESSION_TOKEN Session token for temporary credentials

These can be set globally or scoped per-repository using the env: field.

docs.title: AWS S3
docs.text:
repositories:
  s3-prod:
    url: s3:my-bucket/squirrel
    password: ${env:SQUIRREL_PASSWORD}
    env:
      AWS_ACCESS_KEY_ID: ${env:AWS_ACCESS_KEY_ID}
      AWS_SECRET_ACCESS_KEY: ${env:AWS_SECRET_ACCESS_KEY}
      AWS_DEFAULT_REGION: eu-central-1

docs.title: With IAM Role (EC2 / ECS)
docs.text:
When running on EC2 or ECS with an IAM role, credentials are retrieved automatically from the instance metadata service. No access key variables are needed:

repositories:
  s3-prod:
    url: s3:my-bucket/squirrel
    password: ${env:SQUIRREL_PASSWORD}
    env:
      AWS_DEFAULT_REGION: eu-central-1

docs.title: MinIO
docs.text:
repositories:
  minio:
    url: s3:squirrel-backups/prod
    password: ${env:SQUIRREL_PASSWORD}
    env:
      AWS_ACCESS_KEY_ID: minioadmin
      AWS_SECRET_ACCESS_KEY: ${env:MINIO_SECRET}
      AWS_ENDPOINT_URL: http://minio.example.com:9000

For HTTPS MinIO:

      AWS_ENDPOINT_URL: https://minio.example.com

docs.title: Backblaze B2 (S3-compatible)
docs.text:
repositories:
  b2:
    url: s3:my-b2-bucket/squirrel
    password: ${env:SQUIRREL_PASSWORD}
    env:
      AWS_ACCESS_KEY_ID: ${env:B2_APPLICATION_KEY_ID}
      AWS_SECRET_ACCESS_KEY: ${env:B2_APPLICATION_KEY}
      AWS_ENDPOINT_URL: https://s3.us-west-004.backblazeb2.com
      AWS_DEFAULT_REGION: us-west-004

docs.title: Cloudflare R2
docs.text:
repositories:
  r2:
    url: s3:my-r2-bucket/squirrel
    password: ${env:SQUIRREL_PASSWORD}
    env:
      AWS_ACCESS_KEY_ID: ${env:R2_ACCESS_KEY_ID}
      AWS_SECRET_ACCESS_KEY: ${env:R2_SECRET_ACCESS_KEY}
      AWS_ENDPOINT_URL: https://<account_id>.r2.cloudflarestorage.com

docs.title: Notes
docs.text:
• Bucket must exist before running squirrel init.
  • Enable bucket versioning on AWS S3 for extra protection against accidental deletion.
  • squirrel uses multipart uploads for large pack files.
  • Server-side encryption (SSE-S3 or SSE-KMS) can be enabled on the bucket independently of squirrel's own encryption.

docs.title: SFTP
docs.text: Store repository data on a remote server over SSH File Transfer Protocol (SFTP).

docs.title: URL Format
docs.text:
sftp://user@host/absolute/path
sftp://user:password@host/absolute/path
sftp://user@host:port/path

docs.title: Configuration
docs.text:
repositories:
  sftp-offsite:
    url: sftp://backup@offsite.example.com/backups/squirrel
    password-file: /etc/squirrel/sftp-repo.key

docs.title: SSH Key (recommended)
docs.text:
squirrel uses the SSH key at ~/.ssh/id_rsa (or ~/.ssh/id_ed25519) by default.

repositories:
  sftp-offsite:
    url: sftp://backup@offsite.example.com/backups/squirrel
    password: ${env:SQUIRREL_PASSWORD}

No SSH password is needed if the key is accepted by the server.

docs.title: ssh-agent
docs.text: If SSH_AUTH_SOCK is set, squirrel uses the running ssh-agent automatically.

docs.title: Password in URL
docs.text:
For simple setups (not recommended for production):

repositories:
  sftp-dev:
    url: sftp://backup:sshpassword@dev.example.com/backups/squirrel
    password: ${env:SQUIRREL_PASSWORD}

docs.title: Custom Port
docs.text:
repositories:
  sftp-offsite:
    url: sftp://backup@offsite.example.com:2222/backups/squirrel
    password-file: /etc/squirrel/sftp-repo.key

docs.title: Server Setup
docs.text:
On the remote server, create a dedicated backup user:

useradd -m -s /bin/bash backup
mkdir -p /backups/squirrel
chown backup:backup /backups/squirrel

# Add squirrel host's public key
mkdir -p /home/backup/.ssh
cat squirrel-host.pub >> /home/backup/.ssh/authorized_keys
chmod 600 /home/backup/.ssh/authorized_keys

The backup user only needs read/write access to the backup directory — no shell access is required (can use rssh or scponly as the shell).

docs.title: Notes
docs.text:
• The remote directory must exist before running squirrel init, or the backup user must have permission to create it.
  • squirrel uses write-then-rename for atomic operations over SFTP.
  • SFTP is typically slower than object storage due to per-file round-trip overhead; consider parallelism settings accordingly.
  • SSH host key verification is performed using ~/.ssh/known_hosts.

docs.title: Commands
docs.text:
squirrel uses a hierarchical command structure: squirrel <command> [subcommand] [flags]

All commands that open a repository require either an interactive password prompt or a secret resolved from config.

docs.title: Global Flags
docs.text: Flag Type Description --help bool Show help for the command --version bool Print version information

docs.title: Command Groups
docs.text:
• Repository

    Command Description init Initialize a new encrypted repository check Verify repository integrity stats Repository statistics and dedup ratio prune Remove unreferenced blobs

  • Backup

    Command Description backup Back up files or directories backup postgres PostgreSQL base backup + WAL backup mysql MySQL/MariaDB logical or physical backup

  • Restore

    Command Description restore Restore files from a snapshot restore postgres Restore PostgreSQL base + WAL restore mysql Restore MySQL dump or physical backup

  • Snapshots

    Command Description snapshots List snapshots forget Apply retention policy diff Diff between two snapshots mount Mount snapshot as FUSE filesystem

  • Keys & Secrets

    Command Description key Add / remove / list keys secrets Manage OS keyring secrets

  • Automation

    Command Description config Init / validate / show config run Execute config profiles daemon Run as background scheduler schedule Manage OS-level schedules

  • Utilities

    Command Description pg PostgreSQL utilities self-update Update squirrel to latest release completion Generate shell completions

docs.title: Snapshot ID Prefixes
docs.text: All commands that accept a snapshot ID accept either the full 64-character SHA-256 ID or a unique prefix (e.g. abc12345).

docs.title: squirrel backup mysql
docs.text:
Back up a MySQL or MariaDB server via logical dump, physical data-directory copy, or binlog streaming.

squirrel backup mysql --repo <url> --dsn <dsn> [flags]

docs.title: Flags
docs.text: Flag Type Default Description --repo string — Required. Repository URL or path. --dsn string — Required. MySQL DSN. Can also be set via SQUIRREL_MYSQL_DSN. --database []string all Databases to include in the dump. Can be repeated. If omitted, all databases are backed up. --tag []string — Tags to attach to the snapshot. Can be repeated. --binlog-only bool false Stream binlog events only — skip the full dump. Requires an existing dump snapshot. --physical bool false Physical backup: copy the MySQL data directory instead of dumping SQL. --data-dir string — MySQL data directory path (required when --physical is set).

docs.title: DSN Format
docs.text:
MySQL DSN in Go driver format:

user:password@tcp(host:port)/
user:password@tcp(host:port)/dbname
root:secret@tcp(localhost:3306)/

docs.title: Logical Dump (default)
docs.text:
squirrel connects to MySQL, issues FLUSH TABLES WITH READ LOCK, captures the current binlog position, starts a consistent transaction, and dumps all specified databases as SQL. The lock is released immediately after the binlog position is recorded.

A mysql-dump snapshot is created containing: - SQL dump blobs - binlog_file and binlog_pos (or gtid_set for GTID-enabled servers)

docs.title: Binlog-Only (--binlog-only)
docs.text:
Streams binlog events from the server using MySQL replication protocol, starting from the position recorded in the latest dump snapshot. Each 16 MiB of binlog data is stored as a mysql-binlog snapshot.

Press Ctrl-C to stop streaming.

docs.title: Physical Backup (--physical)
docs.text:
Acquires FLUSH TABLES WITH READ LOCK, copies the MySQL data directory, then releases the lock. Creates a mysql-physical snapshot.

Warning

Physical backup requires direct filesystem access to the MySQL data directory. Typically only possible on the same host as MySQL.

docs.title: Logical dump of specific databases
docs.text:
squirrel backup mysql \
  --dsn "root:secret@tcp(localhost:3306)/" \
  --repo /backup/mysql-repo \
  --database myapp \
  --database auditdb \
  --tag daily

docs.title: Backup all databases
docs.text:
squirrel backup mysql \
  --dsn "root:secret@tcp(localhost:3306)/" \
  --repo /backup/mysql-repo

docs.title: Binlog-only streaming
docs.text:
squirrel backup mysql \
  --dsn "root:secret@tcp(localhost:3306)/" \
  --repo /backup/mysql-repo \
  --binlog-only

docs.title: Physical backup
docs.text:
squirrel backup mysql \
  --dsn "root:secret@tcp(localhost:3306)/" \
  --repo /backup/mysql-repo \
  --physical \
  --data-dir /var/lib/mysql

docs.title: Config profile with GTID
docs.text:
profiles:
  mysql-prod:
    extends: _base-retention
    repository: s3-prod
    type: mysql
    dsn: ${sops-secrets:mysql/prod/dsn}
    databases:
      - appdb
      - auditdb
    tags: [mysql, production]
    schedule: "0 1 * * *"
    hooks:
      post-success:
        - webhook: https://monitoring.example.com/healthcheck/mysql-prod

docs.title: Snapshot Types Created
docs.text: Snapshot Type Created When mysql-dump After each successful logical dump mysql-binlog After each 16 MiB binlog segment mysql-physical After each physical data-dir backup

docs.title: mysql-dump metadata
docs.text:
{
  "binlog_file": "mysql-bin.000003",
  "binlog_pos": 1234,
  "gtid_set": "abc123:1-100"
}

docs.title: mysql-binlog metadata
docs.text:
{
  "segments": ["mysql-bin.000003", "mysql-bin.000004"],
  "dump_snapshot": "abc12345..."
}

docs.title: mysql-physical metadata
docs.text:
{
  "binlog_file": "mysql-bin.000003",
  "binlog_pos": 1234,
  "gtid_set": "abc123:1-100",
  "data_dir": "/var/lib/mysql"
}

docs.title: MySQL / MariaDB Requirements
docs.text:
• MySQL 5.7 / 8.x or MariaDB 10.5+
  • Binary logging enabled: log_bin = ON
  • User privileges: 
    GRANT RELOAD, REPLICATION SLAVE, REPLICATION CLIENT, SELECT ON *.* TO 'squirrel'@'%';
  • For GTID streaming: 
    gtid_mode = ON
enforce_gtid_consistency = ON

docs.title: squirrel backup postgres
docs.text:
Perform a PostgreSQL physical base backup and/or continuous WAL streaming.

squirrel backup postgres --repo <url> --dsn <dsn> [flags]

squirrel connects via the PostgreSQL replication protocol, streams the base backup as a TAR archive, and stores the chunks as deduplicated blobs. WAL segments are captured continuously until interrupted.

docs.title: Flags
docs.text: Flag Type Default Description --repo string — Required. Repository URL or path. --dsn string — Required. PostgreSQL connection string (DSN). Can also be set via SQUIRREL_PG_DSN. --slot string squirrel Replication slot name. Created automatically if it does not exist. --tag []string — Tags to attach to each snapshot. Can be repeated. --wal-only bool false Stream WAL only — skip the base backup. Requires an existing base snapshot.

docs.title: Full Backup Mode (default)
docs.text:
1. Opens a replication connection.
  2. Calls START_REPLICATION / BASE_BACKUP to stream base.tar (pg_data) and any tablespace TARs.
  3. Stores each TAR in deduplicated blobs; saves a postgres-base snapshot with start_lsn, system_id, and timeline.
  4. Switches to WAL streaming from start_lsn and captures segments until Ctrl-C.
  5. Each completed WAL segment (16 MiB by default) is stored as a postgres-wal snapshot.

docs.title: WAL-Only Mode (--wal-only)
docs.text: Skips step 2–3 and starts streaming WAL from the LSN of the latest base snapshot. Use this to run a dedicated WAL archiver alongside periodic base backups.

docs.title: DSN Format
docs.text:
Standard PostgreSQL connection string or URI:

# URI format
postgres://user:password@host:5432/dbname?sslmode=disable

# Key=value format
host=localhost port=5432 user=squirrel password=secret dbname=postgres sslmode=disable

sslmode

If your PostgreSQL server does not have TLS configured (e.g. a local Docker container), add ?sslmode=disable to the URI, otherwise the connection will fail.

docs.title: Replication Slot
docs.text:
squirrel uses a physical replication slot to track WAL progress. The slot is created automatically on the first backup if it does not exist.

Slots retain WAL on the primary — if squirrel stops streaming for a long time, WAL accumulates on disk. Drop unused slots with:

squirrel pg drop-slot --dsn <dsn> --slot squirrel

docs.title: Full backup (base + WAL until Ctrl-C)
docs.text:
squirrel backup postgres \
  --dsn "postgres://squirrel:secret@localhost/postgres?sslmode=disable" \
  --repo /backup/pg-repo \
  --slot squirrel \
  --tag production

docs.title: WAL-only streaming
docs.text:
squirrel backup postgres \
  --dsn "postgres://squirrel:secret@localhost/postgres?sslmode=disable" \
  --repo /backup/pg-repo \
  --slot squirrel \
  --wal-only

docs.title: With S3 backend
docs.text:
export AWS_ACCESS_KEY_ID=...
export AWS_SECRET_ACCESS_KEY=...

squirrel backup postgres \
  --dsn "${PG_DSN}" \
  --repo s3:mybucket/pg-backup \
  --slot prod-slot \
  --tag prod

docs.title: In config profile
docs.text:
profiles:
  pg-prod:
    type: postgres
    repository: s3-prod
    dsn: ${vault-prod:secret/postgres#replication_url}
    slot: squirrel
    tags: [postgres, production]
    schedule: "0 3 * * *"
    hooks:
      post-success:
        - webhook: https://monitoring.example.com/healthcheck/pg-prod

docs.title: Snapshot Types Created
docs.text: Snapshot Type Created When postgres-base After each successful base backup postgres-wal After each 16 MiB WAL segment is flushed

docs.title: PostgreSQL Requirements
docs.text:
• PostgreSQL 12 or later
  • A replication user: 
    CREATE USER squirrel REPLICATION LOGIN PASSWORD 'secret';
GRANT pg_read_all_data TO squirrel;  -- PostgreSQL 14+
  • wal_level = replica (or logical) in postgresql.conf
  • Sufficient max_wal_senders (at least 2)
  • Sufficient max_replication_slots (at least 1)

docs.title: pg_hba.conf
docs.text:
Allow the squirrel host to connect for replication:

host  replication  squirrel  10.0.0.0/8  scram-sha-256

docs.title: PostgreSQL 15+ Protocol (V2)
docs.text:
squirrel automatically detects PostgreSQL 15+ and handles the V2 BASE_BACKUP streaming protocol, which adds a 1-byte type prefix to each CopyData message:

Prefix byte Meaning n (0x6E) Tablespace header — skipped d (0x64) TAR data payload e (0x65) End-of-tablespace marker — skipped

Older PostgreSQL (≤ 14) uses the raw TAR byte stream without type prefixes. Both protocols are handled transparently.

docs.title: squirrel backup
docs.text:
Back up one or more files or directories into a squirrel repository.

squirrel backup --repo <url> --path <path> [flags]

Files are chunked with Rabin CDC, compressed with zstd, encrypted with AES-256-GCM, and stored as content-addressed blobs. Identical content is deduplicated automatically.

docs.title: Flags
docs.text: Flag Type Default Description --repo string — Required. Repository URL or path. --path string — Required. File or directory to back up. Can be specified multiple times. --tag []string — Tags to attach to the snapshot. Can be repeated: --tag prod --tag nightly. --dry-run bool false Show what would be uploaded without writing any data. --parallel int 0 (= CPU count) Number of parallel file upload workers.

docs.title: Excludes
docs.text:
Exclude patterns are specified per-profile in the config file using the excludes field. They are evaluated as glob patterns relative to the backup path.

Common excludes:

profiles:
  my-backup:
    excludes:
      - "**/.git"
      - "**/node_modules"
      - "**/__pycache__"
      - "**/*.tmp"
      - "**/*.log"

docs.title: Basic backup
docs.text: squirrel backup --repo /backup/myrepo --path /home/user/documents

docs.title: Multiple paths
docs.text:
squirrel backup --repo /backup/myrepo \
  --path /home/user/documents \
  --path /etc \
  --path /var/www

docs.title: With tags
docs.text:
squirrel backup --repo /backup/myrepo \
  --path /home/user \
  --tag daily \
  --tag prod

docs.title: Dry run
docs.text:
squirrel backup --repo /backup/myrepo --path /home/user --dry-run

Expected output: 

[dry-run] No data was written.

docs.title: Parallel uploads
docs.text: squirrel backup --repo s3:mybucket/repo --path /data --parallel 8

docs.title: Snapshot Output
docs.text:
A successful backup prints a snapshot ID and statistics:

Snapshot a1b2c3d4 saved  (128 blobs, 512 MiB, 14 MiB new)
  • blobs — total blobs in this snapshot
  • MiB — total logical size
  • new — bytes actually uploaded (rest was deduplicated)

docs.title: Deduplication
docs.text:
On subsequent backups of the same or similar data, squirrel checks the in-session pending map and the persistent index before uploading a blob. Only new or changed content is transmitted.

Snapshot b2c3d4e5 saved  (128 blobs, 512 MiB, 0 MiB new)
#                                                ^^^^^^^^^^
#                                         fully deduplicated

docs.title: Notes
docs.text:
• The snapshot contains the hostname, timestamp, paths, tags, and a tree of blob IDs.
  • File permissions and modification times are preserved.
  • Symlinks and hardlinks are stored correctly.
  • The snapshot ID is deterministic based on content — identical backups produce the same tree.

docs.title: squirrel check
docs.text:
Verify the integrity of a repository by checking all blob checksums.

squirrel check --repo <url>

docs.title: Flags
docs.text: Flag Type Required Description --repo string Yes Repository URL or path.

docs.title: What Is Checked
docs.text:
1. Index consistency — All blob IDs in the index can be located in pack files.
  2. Blob checksums — Each blob is decrypted and its SHA-256 is verified against the stored ID.
  3. Snapshot references — All blobs referenced by snapshots exist in the index.

docs.title: Examples
docs.text:
squirrel check --repo /backup/myrepo

Expected output on success:

repository OK

Output on failure:

ERROR: blob a1b2c3... failed checksum verification

docs.title: Notes
docs.text:
• check reads and decrypts every blob in the repository — it can take a significant amount of time on large repositories.
  • Use it periodically to detect silent data corruption or storage-level bit rot.
  • check is read-only and does not modify the repository.

docs.title: squirrel completion
docs.text:
Generate shell completion scripts for squirrel.

squirrel completion <shell>

docs.title: Supported Shells
docs.text: Shell Argument Bash bash Zsh zsh Fish fish PowerShell powershell

docs.title: Installation
docs.text:
BashZshFishPowerShell

System-wide: 

squirrel completion bash | sudo tee /etc/bash_completion.d/squirrel

User-level: 

mkdir -p ~/.local/share/bash-completion/completions
squirrel completion bash > ~/.local/share/bash-completion/completions/squirrel

Reload your shell or run: 

source /etc/bash_completion.d/squirrel


squirrel completion zsh > "${fpath[1]}/_squirrel"

Or add to ~/.zshrc: 

source <(squirrel completion zsh)


squirrel completion fish > ~/.config/fish/completions/squirrel.fish

Add to your $PROFILE: 

squirrel completion powershell | Out-String | Invoke-Expression

Or load on-demand: 

squirrel completion powershell | Out-String | Invoke-Expression

docs.title: What Completions Provide
docs.text:
• Subcommand names (backup, restore, snapshots, forget, …)
  • Flag names and their types
  • Snapshot ID completion (where applicable)
  • Shell-specific completion (tab, double-tab, etc.)

docs.title: squirrel config
docs.text: Manage the squirrel configuration file.

docs.title: Default Config File Location
docs.text:
Platform Path Linux ~/.config/squirrel/config.yml macOS ~/.config/squirrel/config.yml Windows %APPDATA%\squirrel\config.yml

All subcommands accept --config <path> to override the default location.

docs.title: squirrel config init
docs.text:
Create an initial config file from a template.

squirrel config init [--config <path>] [--force]
Flag Type Default Description --config string platform default Output path for the config file. --force bool false Overwrite existing file.

Creates a commented template with example sections for repositories, secrets, and profiles.

docs.title: squirrel config validate
docs.text:
Parse and validate a config file, reporting any errors.

squirrel config validate [--config <path>]
Flag Type Default Description --config string platform default Config file to validate.

Output:

Config valid.

or:

ERROR: profiles.pg-prod: repository "s3-prod" not defined

docs.title: squirrel config show
docs.text:
Show the fully resolved configuration for a profile, including expanded secret values.

squirrel config show <profile> [--config <path>] [--reveal]
Flag Type Default Description --config string platform default Config file to read. --reveal bool false Show secret values in plain text. Without this flag, secrets are masked as ***. Argument Description <profile> Profile name to display.

docs.title: Create a starter config
docs.text: squirrel config init

docs.title: Validate after editing
docs.text: squirrel config validate

docs.title: Inspect a profile
docs.text:
squirrel config show pg-prod

Sample output (secrets masked):

repository: s3-prod
type: postgres
dsn: ***
slot: squirrel
tags: [postgres, production]
schedule: "0 3 * * *"
retention:
  keep-last: 5
  keep-daily: 14
  prune: true

docs.title: Reveal secrets for debugging
docs.text:
squirrel config show pg-prod --reveal

Warning

--reveal prints secrets in plain text to stdout. Use with caution in shared environments or CI logs.

docs.title: Use custom config path
docs.text:
squirrel config validate --config /etc/squirrel/config.yml
squirrel config show pg-prod --config /etc/squirrel/config.yml

docs.title: squirrel daemon
docs.text:
Run squirrel as a long-running background process with a built-in cron scheduler. Suitable for Docker/container deployments where systemd is unavailable.

squirrel daemon [flags]

docs.title: Flags
docs.text: Flag Type Default Description --config string platform default Path to config file. --profile []string all scheduled Profiles to activate. If omitted, all profiles with a schedule: field are scheduled. --metrics string — Address to expose Prometheus metrics on (e.g. :9090). If omitted, metrics are disabled.

docs.title: How It Works
docs.text:
1. Reads the config file and builds a schedule for each profile that has a schedule: field.
  2. Runs until a SIGTERM or SIGINT is received.
  3. On SIGHUP, reloads the config file without restarting (live reload).
  4. Executes squirrel run <profile> for each profile at the scheduled time.
  5. Exposes Prometheus metrics if --metrics is set.

docs.title: Run all scheduled profiles
docs.text: squirrel daemon

docs.title: Run specific profiles only
docs.text: squirrel daemon --profile pg-daily --profile files-home

docs.title: With Prometheus metrics
docs.text: squirrel daemon --metrics :9090

docs.title: Docker Compose
docs.text:
services:
  squirrel:
    image: ghcr.io/elpol4k0/squirrel:latest
    command: daemon --metrics :9090
    environment:
      SQUIRREL_PASSWORD: ${SQUIRREL_PASSWORD}
    volumes:
      - ./config.yml:/root/.config/squirrel/config.yml:ro
      - squirrel-data:/backup
    ports:
      - "9090:9090"
    restart: unless-stopped

docs.title: Custom config path
docs.text: squirrel daemon --config /etc/squirrel/config.yml --metrics :9090

docs.title: Config Reload
docs.text:
Send SIGHUP to reload the config without downtime:

kill -HUP $(pidof squirrel)

Profiles that are currently running are not interrupted. New schedules take effect after reload.

docs.title: Prometheus Metrics
docs.text:
When --metrics is configured, metrics are available at:

http://host:9090/metrics

See Monitoring for the full list of exposed metrics.

docs.title: Graceful Shutdown
docs.text:
On SIGTERM or SIGINT (Ctrl-C):

  • No new backup jobs are started.
  • Running backup jobs are allowed to finish.
  • The process exits cleanly.

docs.title: Notes
docs.text:
• daemon runs in the foreground — use your init system or process supervisor to keep it running.
  • For OS-level scheduling (systemd, launchd, Task Scheduler), see squirrel schedule.
  • Each profile's schedule: field uses standard cron syntax or shortcuts like @daily.

docs.title: squirrel diff
docs.text:
Show the differences between two snapshots.

squirrel diff <snapshot-a> <snapshot-b> --repo <url> [flags]

docs.title: Flags
docs.text: Flag Type Required Description --repo string Yes Repository URL or path. --stat bool No Show only a summary (counts of added/removed/modified files).

docs.title: Arguments
docs.text: Argument Description <snapshot-a> First snapshot ID or prefix (older). <snapshot-b> Second snapshot ID or prefix (newer).

docs.title: Output
docs.text:
Each line shows the change status and path:

+ /home/user/new-file.txt
- /home/user/deleted-file.txt
M /home/user/changed-file.txt
Prefix Meaning + File added in snapshot B - File removed in snapshot B M File modified between the two snapshots

docs.title: Full diff
docs.text: squirrel diff abc12345 b2c3d4e5 --repo /backup/myrepo

docs.title: Summary only
docs.text:
squirrel diff abc12345 b2c3d4e5 --repo /backup/myrepo --stat

Sample stat output:

Added:    3 files
Removed:  1 file
Modified: 12 files

docs.title: Notes
docs.text:
• Both snapshots must be of type files. Diffing database snapshots is not supported.
  • Snapshot A is typically older (the baseline); snapshot B is newer (the comparison).
  • Order matters: swapping A and B reverses the +/- symbols.

docs.title: squirrel forget
docs.text:
Apply a retention policy to remove old snapshots. Optionally prune unreferenced blobs afterwards.

squirrel forget --repo <url> [retention flags] [flags]

At least one --keep-* flag must be specified.

docs.title: Flags
docs.text: Flag Type Default Description --repo string — Required. Repository URL or path. --keep-last int — Keep the N most recent snapshots regardless of age. --keep-hourly int — Keep the last N hours that have at least one snapshot. --keep-daily int — Keep the last N days that have at least one snapshot. --keep-weekly int — Keep the last N weeks that have at least one snapshot. --keep-monthly int — Keep the last N months that have at least one snapshot. --keep-yearly int — Keep the last N years that have at least one snapshot. --prune bool false Run prune automatically after removing snapshots. --dry-run bool false Show which snapshots would be removed without deleting anything.

docs.title: Retention Policy Logic
docs.text:
Snapshots are evaluated from newest to oldest. A snapshot is kept if it is the first snapshot in its retention bucket that has not yet been filled:

  1. keep-last — The N newest snapshots are always kept, regardless of other rules.
  2. keep-hourly — For each of the last N calendar hours, keep the newest snapshot in that hour.
  3. keep-daily — For each of the last N calendar days, keep the newest snapshot on that day.
  4. keep-weekly — For each of the last N calendar weeks (Monday-based), keep the newest.
  5. keep-monthly — For each of the last N calendar months, keep the newest.
  6. keep-yearly — For each of the last N calendar years, keep the newest.

Snapshots not selected by any rule are removed.

docs.title: Keep last 7 snapshots
docs.text: squirrel forget --repo /backup/myrepo --keep-last 7

docs.title: Daily / weekly / monthly policy
docs.text:
squirrel forget --repo /backup/myrepo \
  --keep-daily 30 \
  --keep-weekly 12 \
  --keep-monthly 24

docs.title: Dry run to preview
docs.text:
squirrel forget --repo /backup/myrepo --keep-last 3 --dry-run

Sample output:

Would remove: b2c3d4e5  2026-04-23 02:00  files
Would remove: c3d4e5f6  2026-04-22 02:00  files
Keep:         abc12345  2026-04-25 02:00  files
Keep:         d4e5f6a7  2026-04-24 02:00  files
Keep:         e5f6a7b8  2026-04-23 03:00  files

docs.title: Forget and prune in one step
docs.text:
squirrel forget --repo /backup/myrepo \
  --keep-last 5 \
  --keep-daily 30 \
  --prune

docs.title: In config profile
docs.text:
profiles:
  files-home:
    retention:
      keep-last: 7
      keep-daily: 30
      keep-weekly: 12
      prune: true

When prune: true is set in the profile, squirrel run will prune after each backup automatically.

docs.title: Notes
docs.text:
• forget only removes snapshot manifests. Blob data is not freed until squirrel prune runs.
  • Use --prune to combine both operations in a single command.
  • Retention policies apply per snapshot type independently; WAL snapshots and base snapshots each have their own retention history.

docs.title: squirrel init
docs.text:
Initialize a new squirrel repository at the given location.

squirrel init --repo <url>

init creates the directory structure, generates a 256-bit master key, wraps it with Argon2id using the password you supply, and stores the result in keys/. The repository is ready to use immediately.

docs.title: Flags
docs.text: Flag Type Required Description --repo string Yes Repository URL or path. See Storage Backends for supported URL schemes.

docs.title: Repository Structure Created
docs.text:
<repo>/
├── config        # Encrypted repository metadata
├── keys/         # One or more password-wrapped master keys
├── data/         # Pack files containing encrypted blobs
├── index/        # Blob ID → pack location mappings
├── snapshots/    # Snapshot manifests
└── locks/        # Concurrency locks

docs.title: Examples
docs.text:
LocalS3SFTPAzure 
squirrel init --repo /backup/myrepo

export AWS_ACCESS_KEY_ID=...
export AWS_SECRET_ACCESS_KEY=...
squirrel init --repo s3:mybucket/squirrel

squirrel init --repo sftp://backup@offsite.example.com/backups/myrepo

export AZURE_STORAGE_ACCOUNT=myaccount
export AZURE_STORAGE_KEY=...
squirrel init --repo az:mycontainer/squirrel

docs.title: Password Entry
docs.text:
squirrel prompts for a password interactively. The password is used to derive a key-encryption key (KEK) via Argon2id (time=3, mem=64 MiB, threads=4), which wraps the master key.

To automate this (e.g. in scripts), source the password from a secret provider in config.yml:

repositories:
  prod:
    url: s3:mybucket/prod
    password: ${env:SQUIRREL_PASSWORD}

See Secret Providers for all options.

docs.title: Notes
docs.text:
• The target path must be empty or non-existent; init will not overwrite an existing repository.
  • Multiple passwords can be added later with squirrel key add.
  • The master key never leaves the repository in plaintext — only password-wrapped copies are stored.

docs.title: squirrel key
docs.text: Manage repository keys (passwords). squirrel supports multiple passwords per repository — all wrap the same master key, so no data re-encryption is needed when adding or removing passwords.

docs.title: squirrel key list
docs.text:
List all keys stored in the repository.

squirrel key list --repo <url>
Flag Type Required Description --repo string Yes Repository URL or path.

Sample output:

ID        Created               Hostname
────────  ────────────────────  ──────────
a1b2c3d4  2026-04-01 10:00:00   myhost     (current)
b2c3d4e5  2026-04-10 12:30:00   myhost

docs.title: squirrel key add
docs.text:
Add a new password to the repository. The new password independently wraps the same master key.

squirrel key add --repo <url>
Flag Type Required Description --repo string Yes Repository URL or path.

squirrel prompts for the existing password to unlock the master key, then prompts for the new password twice.

docs.title: squirrel key remove
docs.text:
Remove a key from the repository by its ID.

squirrel key remove <key-id> --repo <url>
Flag Type Required Description --repo string Yes Repository URL or path. Argument Description <key-id> Key ID from squirrel key list.

Warning

You cannot remove the key you are currently authenticated with. Keep at least one key at all times or the repository becomes inaccessible.

docs.title: Add a second password
docs.text:
squirrel key add --repo /backup/myrepo
# Enter current password:
# Enter new password:
# Confirm new password:

docs.title: List keys
docs.text: squirrel key list --repo /backup/myrepo

docs.title: Remove an old key
docs.text: squirrel key remove b2c3d4e5 --repo /backup/myrepo

docs.title: Password rotation workflow
docs.text:
# 1. Add new password
squirrel key add --repo /backup/myrepo

# 2. Verify the new password works
squirrel snapshots --repo /backup/myrepo
# (enter new password when prompted)

# 3. Remove the old key
squirrel key remove <old-key-id> --repo /backup/myrepo

docs.title: Notes
docs.text:
• All keys wrap the same 256-bit master key — removing a key does not change the master key or require re-encryption.
  • Key IDs are content-addressed (hash of the wrapped key material).
  • The (current) marker in key list indicates which key was used for the current session.

docs.title: squirrel mount
docs.text:
Mount a snapshot as a read-only FUSE filesystem.

squirrel mount <snapshot-id> <mountpoint> --repo <url>

docs.title: Flags
docs.text: Flag Type Required Description --repo string Yes Repository URL or path.

docs.title: Arguments
docs.text: Argument Description <snapshot-id> Snapshot ID or prefix to mount. <mountpoint> Directory to mount the snapshot into. Must exist and be empty.

docs.title: Platform Support
docs.text:
Platform Support Requirement Linux ✓ Native FUSE kernel module (usually pre-installed) macOS ✓ Native macFUSE (install from osxfuse.github.io) Windows ✓ with WinFsp WinFsp must be installed; squirrel must be built with CGO

Windows CGO

On Windows, mount requires squirrel to be built without CGO_ENABLED=0. Install WinFsp first, then rebuild: 

go build -o squirrel.exe .\cmd\squirrel\

docs.title: Examples
docs.text:
Linux / macOSWindows 
mkdir /mnt/snapshot
squirrel mount abc12345 /mnt/snapshot --repo /backup/myrepo

# Browse the snapshot
ls /mnt/snapshot

# Unmount when done
fusermount -u /mnt/snapshot    # Linux
umount /mnt/snapshot           # macOS

# Mount to drive letter
squirrel mount abc12345 S: --repo C:\backup\myrepo

# Or to a directory
squirrel mount abc12345 C:\mnt\snapshot --repo C:\backup\myrepo

docs.title: Use Cases
docs.text:
• Browse a snapshot without a full restore to verify its contents.
  • Restore individual files by copying from the mounted filesystem.
  • Compare a snapshot against the live filesystem.

docs.title: Notes
docs.text:
• The mounted filesystem is read-only — no writes are possible.
  • Press Ctrl-C in the terminal running mount to unmount.
  • FUSE mount decrypts blobs on-demand as files are accessed — it does not download the entire snapshot.

docs.title: squirrel pg
docs.text: PostgreSQL management commands.

docs.title: squirrel pg drop-slot
docs.text:
Drop a PostgreSQL physical replication slot.

squirrel pg drop-slot --dsn <dsn> --slot <name>

docs.title: Flags
docs.text: Flag Type Required Description --dsn string Yes PostgreSQL DSN with replication access. --slot string Yes Name of the replication slot to drop.

docs.title: Why Drop a Slot?
docs.text:
PostgreSQL replication slots retain WAL on disk until the slot is consumed. If squirrel stops streaming WAL for an extended period (e.g. backup job paused), the primary accumulates WAL indefinitely, which can fill the disk.

Drop unused slots to allow WAL cleanup:

squirrel pg drop-slot \
  --dsn "postgres://squirrel:secret@localhost/postgres?sslmode=disable" \
  --slot squirrel

docs.title: Listing Existing Slots
docs.text:
Use psql to list active replication slots:

psql -U squirrel -c "SELECT slot_name, active, restart_lsn FROM pg_replication_slots;"

docs.title: Notes
docs.text:
• Dropping a slot while WAL streaming is active terminates the streaming connection.
  • A new slot is created automatically the next time squirrel backup postgres runs.
  • If the slot does not exist, the command returns an error.

docs.title: squirrel prune
docs.text:
Remove blobs that are no longer referenced by any snapshot and rebuild the repository index.

squirrel prune --repo <url>

docs.title: Flags
docs.text: Flag Type Required Description --repo string Yes Repository URL or path.

docs.title: How It Works
docs.text:
1. Loads all snapshot manifests.
  2. Collects the set of all blob IDs referenced by any live snapshot.
  3. Compares against all blobs in the repository index.
  4. Deletes pack files (or portions thereof) that contain only unreferenced blobs.
  5. Rebuilds and rewrites the index.

docs.title: When to Run
docs.text:
• After squirrel forget to reclaim disk/storage space.
  • Periodically as part of repository maintenance.

Alternatively, set prune: true in a retention policy or use squirrel forget --prune to combine both steps.

docs.title: Manual prune
docs.text:
squirrel prune --repo /backup/myrepo

Sample output:

Pruning unreferenced blobs...
Removed 42 pack files (1.2 GiB freed)
Index rebuilt.

docs.title: Combined forget + prune
docs.text: squirrel forget --repo /backup/myrepo --keep-last 7 --prune

docs.title: Notes
docs.text:
• prune acquires a repository lock to prevent concurrent modifications.
  • Running prune concurrently with an active backup is safe — squirrel uses locks to coordinate.
  • prune does not remove snapshots; use squirrel forget for that.

docs.title: squirrel restore mysql
docs.text:
Restore a MySQL or MariaDB backup from a dump snapshot and optionally extract binlog segments for Point-in-Time Recovery.

squirrel restore mysql <snapshot-id> --repo <url> [flags]

docs.title: Flags
docs.text: Flag Type Default Description --repo string — Required. Repository URL or path. --dsn string — MySQL DSN for executing the SQL restore. Required unless --target is used. --binlog-dir string /tmp/squirrel-binlogs Directory to extract binlog files into. --sql-only bool false Restore the SQL dump only — do not extract binlogs. --target string — Target directory for physical restore (restores data directory files). --innodb-recovery int 0 InnoDB force recovery level (1–6). Used for physical restores with corruption.

docs.title: Arguments
docs.text: Argument Description <snapshot-id> ID or prefix of the mysql-dump or mysql-physical snapshot to restore.

docs.title: SQL Restore (--sql-only)
docs.text:
Executes the stored SQL dump against the target MySQL server:

squirrel restore mysql abc12345 \
  --repo /backup/mysql-repo \
  --dsn "root:secret@tcp(localhost:3306)/" \
  --sql-only

Use this when you only need to restore to the dump point and do not need binlog replay.

docs.title: SQL Restore + Binlog Extraction (default)
docs.text:
Restores the SQL dump and extracts all binlog segments captured after the dump:

squirrel restore mysql abc12345 \
  --repo /backup/mysql-repo \
  --dsn "root:secret@tcp(localhost:3306)/" \
  --binlog-dir /tmp/binlogs

After restore, replay the binlogs manually:

mysqlbinlog /tmp/binlogs/mysql-bin.000003 \
            /tmp/binlogs/mysql-bin.000004 \
  | mysql -uroot -p

For PITR to a specific point in time:

mysqlbinlog --stop-datetime="2026-04-20 14:30:00" \
  /tmp/binlogs/mysql-bin.* | mysql -uroot -p

docs.title: Physical Restore (--target)
docs.text:
Extracts data directory files from a mysql-physical snapshot:

squirrel restore mysql abc12345 \
  --repo /backup/mysql-repo \
  --target /var/lib/mysql \
  --innodb-recovery 1

After extraction, start MySQL normally. If there is corruption, increase --innodb-recovery.

docs.title: InnoDB Recovery Levels
docs.text:
Level Description 0 Normal (default) 1 Skip undo log rollback for incomplete transactions 2 Skip background threads, prevent redo log rollback 3 Skip corrupt data pages 4 Skip undo log (no rollback) 5 Skip undo log with no consistency guarantees 6 Skip redo log application

Warning

Higher recovery levels bypass data integrity checks. Use only when lower levels fail.

docs.title: Restore dump and extract binlogs
docs.text:
squirrel restore mysql abc12345 \
  --repo s3:mybucket/mysql-backup \
  --dsn "root:secret@tcp(localhost:3306)/" \
  --binlog-dir /tmp/binlogs

docs.title: SQL dump only
docs.text:
squirrel restore mysql abc12345 \
  --repo /backup/mysql-repo \
  --dsn "root:secret@tcp(localhost:3306)/" \
  --sql-only

docs.title: Physical restore with InnoDB recovery
docs.text:
squirrel restore mysql abc12345 \
  --repo /backup/mysql-repo \
  --target /var/lib/mysql \
  --innodb-recovery 1

docs.title: After Physical Restore
docs.text:
# Fix ownership
chown -R mysql:mysql /var/lib/mysql

# Start MySQL
systemctl start mysql

# Check error log
journalctl -u mysql -n 50

docs.title: squirrel restore postgres
docs.text:
Restore a PostgreSQL base backup and WAL segments to a target data directory, optionally configuring Point-in-Time Recovery (PITR).

squirrel restore postgres <snapshot-id> --repo <url> --target <path> [flags]

docs.title: Flags
docs.text: Flag Type Default Description --repo string — Required. Repository URL or path. --target string — Required. Target PostgreSQL data directory (PGDATA). --pitr string — Point-in-time recovery timestamp. Format: "2026-04-20 14:30:00". --pitr-lsn string — Point-in-time recovery target LSN. Format: "0/5000028". --wal-dir string auto Directory to extract WAL segments into. Defaults to a temp directory. --verify bool false Verify WAL segment coverage after restore. Prints segment range.

docs.title: Arguments
docs.text: Argument Description <snapshot-id> ID or prefix of the postgres-base snapshot to restore.

docs.title: How It Works
docs.text:
1. Loads the postgres-base snapshot and extracts base.tar into --target.
  2. Rewrites tablespace symlinks to point to locally extracted copies.
  3. Collects all postgres-wal snapshots created after the base backup (matching system_id and timeline).
  4. Extracts WAL segments into --wal-dir.
  5. Writes recovery.signal and appends to postgresql.auto.conf:
  6. restore_command = 'cp <wal-dir>/%f %p'
  7. recovery_target_time (if --pitr is set)
  8. recovery_target_lsn (if --pitr-lsn is set)
  9. recovery_target_action = 'promote'
  10. Optionally verifies WAL segment count and range.

Start PostgreSQL normally — it will enter recovery mode automatically.

docs.title: Restore to latest (no PITR)
docs.text:
squirrel restore postgres abc12345 \
  --repo /backup/pg-repo \
  --target /var/lib/postgresql/17/main

docs.title: Restore with PITR timestamp
docs.text:
squirrel restore postgres abc12345 \
  --repo s3:mybucket/pg-backup \
  --target /var/lib/postgresql/17/main \
  --pitr "2026-04-20 14:30:00" \
  --verify

docs.title: Restore with PITR LSN
docs.text:
squirrel restore postgres abc12345 \
  --repo s3:mybucket/pg-backup \
  --target /var/lib/postgresql/17/main \
  --pitr-lsn "0/5000028"

docs.title: Custom WAL directory
docs.text:
squirrel restore postgres abc12345 \
  --repo /backup/pg-repo \
  --target /var/lib/postgresql/17/main \
  --wal-dir /mnt/wal-archive

docs.title: After Restore — Starting PostgreSQL
docs.text:
# Fix ownership
chown -R postgres:postgres /var/lib/postgresql/17/main

# Start PostgreSQL
systemctl start postgresql@17-main

# Watch recovery progress
tail -f /var/log/postgresql/postgresql-17-main.log

PostgreSQL will: 1. Enter recovery mode (detects recovery.signal) 2. Replay WAL segments via restore_command 3. Stop at recovery_target_time or recovery_target_lsn (if set) 4. Promote to primary (removes recovery.signal)

docs.title: --verify Output
docs.text:
WAL verified: 42 segment(s), range 000000010000000000000005 .. 00000001000000000000002E

If no WAL segments are found, --verify returns an error.

docs.title: Tablespace Handling
docs.text:
If the original PostgreSQL server had tablespaces, squirrel:

  1. Extracts each tablespace TAR into <target>/pg_tblspc_data/<oid>/
  2. Replaces the symlink in <target>/pg_tblspc/<oid> to point to the local copy

This allows restore to a host with a different filesystem layout than the original server.

docs.title: Generated Configuration
docs.text:
squirrel appends the following to postgresql.auto.conf:

# Written by squirrel
restore_command = 'cp /path/to/wal/%f %p'
recovery_target_time = '2026-04-20 14:30:00'   # only when --pitr is set
recovery_target_action = 'promote'              # only when --pitr or --pitr-lsn is set

And creates an empty recovery.signal file in the data directory.

docs.title: squirrel restore
docs.text:
Restore files from a snapshot into a target directory.

squirrel restore <snapshot-id> --repo <url> --target <path>

docs.title: Flags
docs.text: Flag Type Required Description --repo string Yes Repository URL or path. --target string Yes Directory to restore files into. Created if it does not exist.

docs.title: Arguments
docs.text: Argument Description <snapshot-id> Full or prefix of the snapshot ID to restore. Use squirrel snapshots to find IDs.

docs.title: Behavior
docs.text:
• Files are restored with original permissions and modification times.
  • Symlinks and hardlinks are recreated.
  • The target directory is created if it does not exist.
  • Existing files in the target are overwritten.

docs.title: Restore to a new directory
docs.text: squirrel restore abc12345 --repo /backup/myrepo --target /restore/data

docs.title: Using a prefix
docs.text: squirrel restore abc1 --repo /backup/myrepo --target /restore/data

docs.title: Restore from S3
docs.text:
squirrel restore abc12345 \
  --repo s3:mybucket/squirrel \
  --target /restore/data

docs.title: Verifying a Restore
docs.text:
After restoring, compare the source and target with standard tools:

Linux / macOSPowerShell 
diff -rq /original/data /restore/data

Compare-Object `
  (Get-ChildItem -Recurse /original/data) `
  (Get-ChildItem -Recurse /restore/data)

docs.title: Notes
docs.text:
• Only files-type snapshots can be restored with this command.
  • For PostgreSQL restores, see squirrel restore postgres.
  • For MySQL restores, see squirrel restore mysql.

docs.title: squirrel run
docs.text:
Execute one or more backup profiles defined in the config file.

squirrel run <profile> [profile...] [flags]

docs.title: Flags
docs.text: Flag Type Default Description --config string platform default Path to config file. --parallel int 1 Number of profiles to run concurrently.

docs.title: Arguments
docs.text: Argument Description <profile> One or more profile names to execute. Must not be abstract profiles (names starting with _ by convention).

docs.title: Profile Execution
docs.text:
For each profile, run performs:

  1. Resolves secrets from all configured providers.
  2. Opens the repository specified by repository:.
  3. Executes the appropriate backup command based on type: (files, postgres, mysql).
  4. Applies the retention policy from retention: (if configured).
  5. Prunes unreferenced blobs if retention.prune: true.
  6. Runs hooks (pre-backup, post-success, post-failure).

docs.title: Run a single profile
docs.text: squirrel run pg-daily

docs.title: Run multiple profiles sequentially
docs.text: squirrel run pg-daily files-home mysql-weekly

docs.title: Run multiple profiles in parallel
docs.text: squirrel run pg-daily files-home --parallel 2

docs.title: Use a custom config file
docs.text: squirrel run pg-daily --config /etc/squirrel/config.yml

docs.title: Exit Codes
docs.text:
Code Meaning 0 All profiles completed successfully. 1 One or more profiles failed.

When --parallel is greater than 1, all profiles run before a non-zero exit code is returned.

docs.title: Environment Variables Available in Hooks
docs.text: Variable Value SQUIRREL_PROFILE Name of the profile being executed SQUIRREL_REPO Repository URL from the profile

docs.title: Notes
docs.text:
• Abstract profiles (with abstract: true) cannot be run directly with squirrel run.
  • Profile inheritance (extends:) is resolved before execution — the child profile receives all parent settings, with its own fields taking precedence.
  • Use squirrel daemon to run profiles on a schedule automatically.

docs.title: squirrel schedule
docs.text:
Install, remove, and list OS-level scheduled tasks for squirrel profiles.

squirrel integrates with the native scheduler on each platform:

Platform Scheduler Linux systemd timers + service units macOS launchd plists Windows Task Scheduler

docs.title: squirrel schedule install
docs.text:
Install an OS-level scheduled task for one or more profiles.

squirrel schedule install <profile> [profile...] [flags]
Flag Type Default Description --config string platform default Path to config file.

The cron expression is taken from the schedule: field in the profile definition.

docs.title: Generated Files
docs.text:
Linux (user)Linux (root)macOSWindows 
~/.config/systemd/user/squirrel-<profile>.service
~/.config/systemd/user/squirrel-<profile>.timer

/etc/systemd/system/squirrel-<profile>.service
/etc/systemd/system/squirrel-<profile>.timer

~/Library/LaunchAgents/io.squirrel.<profile>.plist

Task Scheduler → \squirrel-<profile>

docs.title: squirrel schedule remove
docs.text:
Remove the OS-level scheduled task for one or more profiles.

squirrel schedule remove <profile> [profile...] [flags]
Flag Type Default Description --config string platform default Path to config file.

docs.title: squirrel schedule list
docs.text:
List all installed squirrel scheduled tasks.

squirrel schedule list

docs.title: Install a schedule for pg-daily
docs.text:
# Profile must have a schedule: field
squirrel schedule install pg-daily
Linux (enable timer)macOS 
systemctl --user enable --now squirrel-pg-daily.timer
systemctl --user status squirrel-pg-daily.timer

launchctl load ~/Library/LaunchAgents/io.squirrel.pg-daily.plist

docs.title: List installed schedules
docs.text: squirrel schedule list

docs.title: Remove a schedule
docs.text: squirrel schedule remove pg-daily

docs.title: Cron Expression Format
docs.text:
squirrel uses standard 5-field cron syntax:

┌───── minute (0–59)
│ ┌──── hour (0–23)
│ │ ┌─── day of month (1–31)
│ │ │ ┌── month (1–12)
│ │ │ │ ┌─ day of week (0–7, 0 or 7 = Sunday)
│ │ │ │ │
* * * * *

Shortcuts:

Shortcut Equivalent @hourly 0 * * * * @daily 0 0 * * * @weekly 0 0 * * 0 @monthly 0 0 1 * *

Examples:

schedule: "0 2 * * *"       # Daily at 02:00
schedule: "30 3 * * 0"      # Weekly on Sunday at 03:30
schedule: "0 */6 * * *"     # Every 6 hours
schedule: "@daily"           # Daily at midnight

docs.title: Notes
docs.text:
• The schedule: field is required in the profile for schedule install to work.
  • As an alternative to OS-level scheduling, use squirrel daemon, which has a built-in scheduler suitable for containers.

docs.title: squirrel secrets
docs.text:
Manage secrets stored in the OS keyring (macOS Keychain, Windows Credential Manager, GNOME Keyring / libsecret on Linux).

These secrets are used by the ${keyring:service/key} secret provider in config.yml.

docs.title: squirrel secrets set
docs.text:
Store a secret in the OS keyring.

squirrel secrets set <key> [--service <name>]
Flag Type Default Description --service string squirrel Keyring service namespace. Argument Description <key> Key name under which the secret is stored.

squirrel prompts for the secret value interactively.

docs.title: squirrel secrets list
docs.text:
List all keys stored under a service in the OS keyring.

squirrel secrets list [--service <name>]
Flag Type Default Description --service string squirrel Keyring service namespace.

docs.title: squirrel secrets delete
docs.text:
Delete a secret from the OS keyring.

squirrel secrets delete <key> [--service <name>]
Flag Type Default Description --service string squirrel Keyring service namespace. Argument Description <key> Key name to delete.

docs.title: Store a repository password
docs.text:
squirrel secrets set repo-password
# Enter value:

Then reference it in config.yml:

repositories:
  local:
    url: /backup/myrepo
    password: ${keyring:squirrel/repo-password}

docs.title: Store with a custom service namespace
docs.text:
squirrel secrets set prod-password --service squirrel-prod

secrets:
  keyring-prod:
    type: keyring
    service: squirrel-prod

repositories:
  prod:
    url: s3:mybucket/prod
    password: ${keyring-prod:prod-password}

docs.title: List all stored keys
docs.text: squirrel secrets list

docs.title: Delete a secret
docs.text: squirrel secrets delete repo-password

docs.title: Platform Notes
docs.text:
