Platform Backend macOS Keychain Windows Credential Manager (squirrel/<service>/<key>) Linux GNOME Keyring (via libsecret) or KWallet

On headless Linux servers without a keyring daemon, use the ${env:VAR} or ${file:/path} secret providers instead.

docs.title: squirrel self-update
docs.text:
Update the squirrel binary to the latest release from GitHub.

squirrel self-update [flags]

docs.title: Flags
docs.text: Flag Type Default Description --check bool false Check for a newer version without downloading or installing.

docs.title: Update to latest
docs.text:
squirrel self-update

Sample output:

Current version: v1.2.0
Latest version:  v1.3.0
Downloading v1.3.0...
Updated successfully.

If already on the latest version:

squirrel is up to date (v1.3.0)

docs.title: Check for updates without installing
docs.text: squirrel self-update --check

docs.title: Notes
docs.text:
• self-update replaces the current binary in-place. The running process is not affected; restart squirrel after updating.
  • On Linux/macOS, the binary must be writable by the current user. Run with sudo if it is installed system-wide.
  • When installed via Homebrew, update with brew upgrade elpol4k0/tap/squirrel instead.
  • When running inside Docker, update the image instead: docker pull ghcr.io/elpol4k0/squirrel:latest.

docs.title: squirrel snapshots
docs.text:
List snapshots stored in a repository with optional filtering.

squirrel snapshots --repo <url> [flags]

docs.title: Flags
docs.text: Flag Type Default Description --repo string — Required. Repository URL or path. --host string — Filter snapshots by hostname. --tag string — Filter snapshots by tag. --type string — Filter by snapshot type. See Snapshot Types below. --latest bool false Show only the most recent snapshot (respects other filters). --json bool false Output as JSON array instead of a table.

docs.title: Snapshot Types
docs.text: Type Description files File or directory backup postgres-base PostgreSQL physical base backup postgres-wal PostgreSQL WAL segment archive mysql-dump MySQL/MariaDB logical SQL dump mysql-binlog MySQL/MariaDB binlog segment archive mysql-physical MySQL/MariaDB physical data-directory backup

docs.title: List all snapshots
docs.text:
squirrel snapshots --repo /backup/myrepo

Sample output:

ID        Time                  Host        Tags          Type     Size
────────  ────────────────────  ──────────  ────────────  ───────  ──────
abc12345  2026-04-25 02:00:01   myhost      daily,prod    files    512 MiB
b2c3d4e5  2026-04-24 02:00:02   myhost      daily,prod    files    510 MiB
c3d4e5f6  2026-04-25 03:00:00   myhost      postgres      postgres-base  2.1 GiB

docs.title: Filter by type
docs.text: squirrel snapshots --repo /backup/pg-repo --type postgres-base

docs.title: Filter by tag
docs.text: squirrel snapshots --repo /backup/myrepo --tag prod

docs.title: Most recent snapshot
docs.text: squirrel snapshots --repo /backup/myrepo --latest

docs.title: Most recent of a specific type
docs.text: squirrel snapshots --repo /backup/pg-repo --type postgres-base --latest

docs.title: JSON output
docs.text:
squirrel snapshots --repo /backup/myrepo --json

[
  {
    "id": "abc12345...",
    "time": "2026-04-25T02:00:01Z",
    "hostname": "myhost",
    "paths": ["/home/user/data"],
    "tags": ["daily", "prod"],
    "tree": "blob-id-of-tree",
    "meta": {
      "type": "files"
    }
  }
]

docs.title: Use in scripts (PowerShell)
docs.text:
$latest = (squirrel snapshots --repo C:\backup\repo --type postgres-base --json |
            ConvertFrom-Json)[0].id
squirrel restore postgres $latest --repo C:\backup\repo --target C:\pgdata

docs.title: Use in scripts (Bash)
docs.text:
LATEST=$(squirrel snapshots --repo /backup/pg-repo --type postgres-base --json \
         | jq -r '.[0].id')
squirrel restore postgres "$LATEST" --repo /backup/pg-repo --target /pgdata

docs.title: Snapshot Metadata Fields
docs.text:
Every snapshot contains:

Field Description id Unique content-addressed identifier time Creation timestamp (UTC) hostname Hostname of the machine that created the snapshot paths Paths that were backed up tags User-supplied tags tree Blob ID of the root tree meta.type Snapshot type meta.* Type-specific fields (LSN, binlog position, etc.)

docs.title: squirrel stats
docs.text:
Display repository statistics including snapshot count, blob count, storage size, and optional deduplication ratio.

squirrel stats --repo <url> [flags]

docs.title: Flags
docs.text: Flag Type Default Description --repo string — Required. Repository URL or path. --json bool false Output as JSON. --dedup bool false Compute logical size and dedup ratio. Requires reading all snapshot trees (slower).

docs.title: Basic stats
docs.text:
squirrel stats --repo /backup/myrepo

Sample output:

Snapshots:     12
Blobs:       1842
Pack files:    38
Storage size:  4.2 GiB

docs.title: With dedup ratio
docs.text:
squirrel stats --repo /backup/myrepo --dedup

Sample output:

Snapshots:     12
Blobs:       1842
Pack files:    38
Storage size:  4.2 GiB
Logical size:  18.7 GiB
Dedup ratio:   4.45x

A dedup ratio greater than 1 means data is stored more efficiently than raw; higher values indicate more deduplication.

docs.title: JSON output
docs.text:
squirrel stats --repo /backup/myrepo --json

{
  "snapshots": 12,
  "blobs": 1842,
  "pack_files": 38,
  "storage_bytes": 4509715456,
  "logical_bytes": 20079869952,
  "dedup_ratio": 4.45
}

docs.title: Notes
docs.text:
• --dedup reads every snapshot tree to compute logical size, which may be slow on repositories with many large snapshots.
  • Storage size reflects the actual bytes used on the backend (after compression and encryption).
  • Logical size reflects the total uncompressed, undeduplicated size of all backed-up data across all snapshots.

docs.title: Configuration
docs.text: squirrel is configured via a YAML file. This allows you to define repositories, profiles, retention policies, hooks, and secret providers in one place and run backups with squirrel run <profile>.

docs.title: Config File Location
docs.text:
Platform Default Path Linux ~/.config/squirrel/config.yml macOS ~/.config/squirrel/config.yml Windows %APPDATA%\squirrel\config.yml

Override with --config <path> on any command, or SQUIRREL_CONFIG environment variable.

docs.title: Top-Level Structure
docs.text:
version: 1              # (required) Schema version — always 1

secrets: {}             # Named secret providers (optional)
repositories: {}        # Repository definitions
defaults: {}            # Global defaults applied to all profiles
profiles: {}            # Backup profiles

docs.title: Sections
docs.text: Section Description Reference secrets Named secret providers (Vault, SOPS, keyring, …) Secret Providers repositories Repository URLs, passwords, backend env vars Repositories defaults Retention defaults applied to all profiles Defaults profiles Backup jobs with type, paths, schedule, retention, hooks Profiles

docs.title: Minimal Example
docs.text:
version: 1

repositories:
  local:
    url: /backup/myrepo
    password: ${env:SQUIRREL_PASSWORD}

profiles:
  files-home:
    repository: local
    paths:
      - /home/user/documents
    schedule: "0 2 * * *"
    retention:
      keep-daily: 7
      prune: true

docs.title: Full Example
docs.text: See config.yml.example in the repository for a comprehensive example covering all backends, secret providers, and profile types.

docs.title: Managing the Config
docs.text:
# Create from template
squirrel config init

# Validate syntax and references
squirrel config validate

# Show resolved profile (secrets masked)
squirrel config show pg-daily

# Show with secrets revealed
squirrel config show pg-daily --reveal

docs.title: Global Defaults
docs.text: The defaults: section sets values that apply to all profiles unless overridden.

docs.title: Structure
docs.text:
defaults:
  retention:
    keep-last: 3
    keep-daily: 7
    keep-weekly: 4
    keep-monthly: 12
    keep-yearly: 3
    prune: true

docs.title: Supported Default Fields
docs.text: Field Description retention Default retention policy applied to all profiles. See Retention for all sub-fields.

docs.title: Override Behavior
docs.text: Profile-level retention fields override defaults field-by-field. Unspecified fields in a profile fall back to the default.

docs.title: Example
docs.text:
defaults:
  retention:
    keep-daily: 7
    keep-weekly: 4
    keep-monthly: 12
    prune: true

profiles:
  files-home:
    retention:
      keep-daily: 30    # overrides default keep-daily
      # keep-weekly: 4 from defaults
      # keep-monthly: 12 from defaults
      # prune: true from defaults

  pg-staging:
    retention:
      keep-last: 3
      keep-daily: 7
      prune: true       # explicitly set; defaults are ignored for this profile

docs.title: Notes
docs.text:
• Currently, only retention is supported under defaults:.
  • If no defaults: section is present and no retention is set in a profile, no retention policy is applied — old snapshots accumulate indefinitely.
  • It is good practice to always set a default retention policy to prevent unbounded storage growth.

docs.title: Hooks
docs.text: Hooks run shell commands or HTTP webhooks at defined points in the backup lifecycle.

docs.title: Hook Events
docs.text: Event When it runs pre-backup Before the backup starts post-success After a successful backup (and retention, if configured) post-failure After a failed backup

docs.title: Hook Actions
docs.text: Each hook event accepts a list of actions. Two action types are supported:

docs.title: command
docs.text:
Execute a shell command. Arguments are passed as a list (no shell interpolation).

hooks:
  pre-backup:
    - command: [psql, -c, "CHECKPOINT"]

  post-success:
    - command: [notify-send, "squirrel", "Backup succeeded"]
    - command: [curl, -X, POST, "https://example.com/notify"]

  post-failure:
    - command: [page-oncall, "${SQUIRREL_PROFILE}"]

docs.title: webhook
docs.text:
Send an HTTP POST request to a URL. The body is empty; the URL may include a status code suffix.

hooks:
  post-success:
    - webhook: https://hc-ping.com/abc-123-uuid

  post-failure:
    - webhook: https://hc-ping.com/abc-123-uuid/fail

docs.title: Hook Environment Variables
docs.text:
These variables are available to command hooks:

Variable Value SQUIRREL_PROFILE Profile name being executed SQUIRREL_REPO Repository URL from the profile

docs.title: Full Example
docs.text:
profiles:
  files-home:
    repository: local
    paths:
      - /home/user/documents
    hooks:
      pre-backup:
        - command: [logger, "squirrel: starting files-home backup"]

      post-success:
        - webhook: https://monitoring.example.com/healthcheck/files-home
        - command: [logger, "squirrel: files-home backup succeeded"]

      post-failure:
        - command: [notify-send, "squirrel", "files-home backup FAILED"]
        - webhook: https://alerts.example.com/squirrel/files-home/failed

  pg-prod:
    repository: s3-prod
    type: postgres
    dsn: ${vault-prod:secret/data/postgres#replication_url}
    slot: squirrel
    hooks:
      pre-backup:
        - command: [psql, -c, "CHECKPOINT"]

      post-success:
        - webhook: https://monitoring.example.com/healthcheck/pg-prod

      post-failure:
        - webhook: https://alerts.example.com/pg-prod-backup-failed
        - command: [send-alert, --severity, critical, "--message", "pg-prod backup failed"]

docs.title: Multiple Actions per Event
docs.text: Actions are executed in order. If a command action fails (non-zero exit code), the remaining actions for that event are still executed. Hook failures are logged but do not affect the backup exit code.

docs.title: Notes
docs.text:
• command actions do not use a shell — arguments are passed directly to exec. Use explicit ["sh", "-c", "..."] if you need shell features.
  • webhook actions use HTTP POST with no body and a 10-second timeout. Redirect responses are followed.
  • Hook failures are recorded in logs but do not cause squirrel run to fail.

docs.title: Profiles
docs.text:
Profiles define backup jobs. Each profile specifies what to back up, where to store it, when to run, and how to handle retention and notifications.

profiles:
  <name>:
    repository: <repo-name>
    type: files | postgres | mysql
    # ... type-specific fields
    schedule: "<cron>"
    tags: [tag1, tag2]
    retention: {}
    hooks: {}
    extends: <parent-profile>
    abstract: false

docs.title: Common Fields
docs.text: Field Type Description repository string Required. Name of the repository from the repositories: section. type string Backup type: files, postgres, or mysql. Defaults to files. schedule string Cron expression or shortcut for squirrel daemon / squirrel schedule. tags []string Tags attached to every snapshot created by this profile. retention object Retention policy. See Retention. hooks object Pre/post-backup hooks. See Hooks. extends string Inherit settings from another profile. abstract bool If true, this profile cannot be run directly (only used as a base for inheritance).

docs.title: File Backup Fields
docs.text:
Used when type: files (or type is omitted).

Field Type Description paths []string Required. Paths to back up. excludes []string Glob patterns to exclude. Evaluated relative to each path. 
profiles:
  files-home:
    repository: local
    type: files
    paths:
      - /home/user/documents
      - /home/user/projects
    excludes:
      - "**/.git"
      - "**/node_modules"
      - "**/__pycache__"
      - "**/*.tmp"
    tags: [files, home]
    schedule: "0 2 * * *"

docs.title: PostgreSQL Profile Fields
docs.text:
Used when type: postgres.

Field Type Description dsn string Required. PostgreSQL DSN. Supports ${...} syntax. slot string Replication slot name. Default: squirrel. 
profiles:
  pg-prod:
    repository: s3-prod
    type: postgres
    dsn: ${vault-prod:secret/data/postgres#replication_url}
    slot: squirrel
    tags: [postgres, production]
    schedule: "0 3 * * *"

docs.title: MySQL Profile Fields
docs.text:
Used when type: mysql.

Field Type Description dsn string Required. MySQL DSN. Supports ${...} syntax. databases []string Databases to include. If omitted, all databases are backed up. 
profiles:
  mysql-prod:
    repository: s3-prod
    type: mysql
    dsn: ${sops-secrets:mysql/prod/dsn}
    databases:
      - appdb
      - auditdb
    tags: [mysql, production]
    schedule: "0 1 * * *"

docs.title: Profile Inheritance
docs.text: Profiles can inherit from other profiles using extends:. Child fields override parent fields; maps are merged, not replaced.

docs.title: Abstract Base Profile
docs.text:
profiles:
  _base-retention:
    abstract: true
    retention:
      keep-last: 5
      keep-daily: 14
      keep-weekly: 8
      keep-monthly: 24
      keep-yearly: 5
      prune: true

Abstract profiles are prefixed with _ by convention and cannot be run directly.

docs.title: Child Profile
docs.text:
profiles:
  pg-prod:
    extends: _base-retention
    repository: s3-prod
    type: postgres
    dsn: ${vault-prod:secret/data/postgres#replication_url}
    slot: squirrel
    tags: [postgres, production]
    schedule: "0 3 * * *"
    hooks:
      post-success:
        - webhook: https://monitoring.example.com/healthcheck/pg-prod

The child inherits retention from _base-retention and adds its own repository, type, dsn, slot, tags, schedule, and hooks.

docs.title: Multi-Repo Offsite
docs.text:
A profile can extend another to back up to a second location:

profiles:
  pg-prod-offsite:
    extends: pg-prod
    repository: sftp-offsite
    tags: [postgres, production, offsite]
    schedule: "0 5 * * 0"   # weekly on Sunday at 05:00

docs.title: Complete Profile Example
docs.text:
profiles:
  files-home:
    repository: local
    paths:
      - /home/user/documents
      - /home/user/projects
    excludes:
      - "**/.git"
      - "**/node_modules"
      - "**/__pycache__"
    tags: [files, home]
    schedule: "0 2 * * *"
    retention:
      keep-last: 7
      keep-daily: 30
      keep-weekly: 12
      prune: true
    hooks:
      post-success:
        - webhook: https://monitoring.example.com/healthcheck/files-home
      post-failure:
        - command: [notify-send, "squirrel", "files-home backup FAILED"]

docs.title: Profile Naming
docs.text:
• Use lowercase, hyphen-separated names: pg-prod, files-home, mysql-dev.
  • Abstract profiles are conventionally prefixed with _: _base-retention, _base-hooks.
  • Profile names are passed to squirrel run <name> and exposed as SQUIRREL_PROFILE in hooks.

docs.title: Repositories
docs.text:
The repositories section defines named repository targets that profiles reference by name.

repositories:
  <name>:
    url: <backend-url>
    password: <secret>          # or
    password-file: <path>
    env:
      KEY: value

docs.title: Fields
docs.text:
Field Type Description url string Required. Backend URL or path. See Storage Backends. password string Repository password. Supports secret provider syntax ${...}. password-file string Path to a file containing the repository password. env map[string]string Extra environment variables injected when accessing this repository. Values support ${...} syntax.

Exactly one of password or password-file must be set (or an interactive prompt is used).

docs.title: Backend URL Schemes
docs.text: Scheme Example Local filesystem /backup/myrepo or file:///backup/myrepo Amazon S3 / MinIO s3:bucket/prefix Google Cloud Storage gs:bucket/prefix Azure Blob Storage az:container/prefix SFTP sftp://user@host/path

docs.title: Local filesystem
docs.text:
repositories:
  local:
    url: /backup/squirrel-repo
    password: ${env:SQUIRREL_PASSWORD}

docs.title: Amazon S3
docs.text:
repositories:
  s3-prod:
    url: s3:my-bucket/squirrel/prod
    password: ${sops-secrets:repos/s3-prod/password}
    env:
      AWS_ACCESS_KEY_ID: ${env:AWS_ACCESS_KEY_ID}
      AWS_SECRET_ACCESS_KEY: ${env:AWS_SECRET_ACCESS_KEY}
      AWS_DEFAULT_REGION: eu-central-1

docs.title: MinIO (S3-compatible)
docs.text:
repositories:
  minio:
    url: s3:squirrel-backups/prod
    password: ${env:SQUIRREL_PASSWORD}
    env:
      AWS_ACCESS_KEY_ID: minioadmin
      AWS_SECRET_ACCESS_KEY: ${env:MINIO_SECRET}
      AWS_ENDPOINT_URL: http://minio.example.com:9000

docs.title: Azure Blob Storage
docs.text:
repositories:
  azure:
    url: az:mycontainer/squirrel
    password: ${vault-prod:secret/squirrel#azure_repo_password}
    env:
      AZURE_STORAGE_ACCOUNT: mystorageaccount
      AZURE_STORAGE_KEY: ${env:AZURE_STORAGE_KEY}

docs.title: Google Cloud Storage
docs.text:
repositories:
  gcs:
    url: gs:my-gcs-bucket/squirrel
    password: ${keyring-prod:repo-password}
    env:
      GOOGLE_APPLICATION_CREDENTIALS: /etc/squirrel/gcs-sa.json

docs.title: SFTP with key file
docs.text:
repositories:
  sftp-offsite:
    url: sftp://backup@offsite.example.com/backups/squirrel
    password-file: /etc/squirrel/sftp-repo.key

docs.title: Multiple repositories
docs.text:
repositories:
  primary:
    url: s3:primary-bucket/squirrel
    password: ${env:SQUIRREL_PASSWORD}
    env:
      AWS_ACCESS_KEY_ID: ${env:AWS_PRIMARY_KEY}
      AWS_SECRET_ACCESS_KEY: ${env:AWS_PRIMARY_SECRET}

  offsite:
    url: sftp://backup@offsite.example.com/backups/squirrel
    password: ${env:SQUIRREL_PASSWORD}

docs.title: Notes
docs.text:
• Repository names must be unique within the config file.
  • env values are injected into the process environment before the backend driver initializes.
  • Secret references in env values are resolved before injection.
  • The same physical repository can be listed under multiple names with different credentials.

docs.title: Retention Policies
docs.text: Retention policies control how many snapshots are kept. squirrel implements a bucket-based strategy that matches the approach used by restic and Borg.

docs.title: Retention Fields
docs.text:
retention:
  keep-last: 5        # Keep N most recent snapshots
  keep-hourly: 24     # Keep 1 per hour for N hours
  keep-daily: 7       # Keep 1 per day for N days
  keep-weekly: 4      # Keep 1 per week for N weeks
  keep-monthly: 12    # Keep 1 per month for N months
  keep-yearly: 3      # Keep 1 per year for N years
  prune: true         # Delete unreferenced blobs after applying policy

All fields are optional. At least one keep-* must be set to use a retention policy.

docs.title: How the Policy Works
docs.text:
Snapshots are evaluated from newest to oldest. For each bucket type, the first snapshot in each time period (calendar hour, day, week, month, year) is kept. All others in that period are dropped.

keep-last always keeps the N newest snapshots regardless of which buckets they fill.

docs.title: Example
docs.text:
With keep-last: 3, keep-daily: 7, keep-weekly: 4:

  • The 3 newest snapshots are always kept.
  • For each of the last 7 calendar days, the newest snapshot in that day is kept.
  • For each of the last 4 calendar weeks (Mon–Sun), the newest snapshot in that week is kept.
  • Any snapshot not selected by any rule is removed.

docs.title: prune Flag
docs.text:
retention:
  keep-last: 7
  prune: true

When prune: true is set, squirrel automatically runs squirrel prune after applying the retention policy. This frees storage by removing blob data that no longer belongs to any snapshot.

Without prune: true, only snapshot manifests are removed; blob data remains until squirrel prune is run manually.

docs.title: Global Defaults
docs.text:
Set default retention applied to all profiles in defaults::

defaults:
  retention:
    keep-last: 3
    keep-daily: 7
    keep-weekly: 4
    keep-monthly: 12
    keep-yearly: 3
    prune: true

Individual profiles override these defaults field-by-field:

profiles:
  files-home:
    retention:
      keep-daily: 30    # overrides defaults.retention.keep-daily
      # keep-weekly, keep-monthly, keep-yearly from defaults still apply

docs.title: Applying Retention Manually
docs.text:
# Preview what would be removed
squirrel forget --repo /backup/myrepo \
  --keep-last 5 --keep-daily 30 --dry-run

# Apply and prune
squirrel forget --repo /backup/myrepo \
  --keep-last 5 --keep-daily 30 --prune

docs.title: Minimal (development / staging)
docs.text:
retention:
  keep-last: 3
  keep-daily: 7
  prune: true

docs.title: Standard (production)
docs.text:
retention:
  keep-last: 5
  keep-daily: 14
  keep-weekly: 8
  keep-monthly: 24
  keep-yearly: 5
  prune: true

docs.title: Long-term compliance
docs.text:
retention:
  keep-daily: 30
  keep-monthly: 36
  keep-yearly: 7
  prune: true

docs.title: Secret Providers
docs.text: squirrel resolves secrets at runtime using a ${provider:path} syntax. This keeps sensitive values (passwords, tokens, DSNs) out of the config file.

docs.title: Inline Secret Syntax
docs.text:
Any config field that accepts a string can use:

${provider:path}
${provider:path#field}
${named-provider:key}

The provider is either a built-in (resolved directly) or a named provider defined in the secrets: section.

docs.title: Built-In Providers
docs.text: These work without a secrets: entry.

docs.title: ${env:VAR}
docs.text:
Read from an environment variable.

password: ${env:SQUIRREL_PASSWORD}
dsn: ${env:PG_DSN}

docs.title: ${file:/path}
docs.text:
Read the contents of a file (whitespace is trimmed).

password: ${file:/etc/squirrel/repo.key}

docs.title: ${keyring:service/key}
docs.text:
Read from the OS keyring. Service defaults to squirrel.

password: ${keyring:squirrel/repo-password}

Store with: squirrel secrets set repo-password

docs.title: ${cmd:command arg1 arg2}
docs.text:
Execute a command and use its stdout as the secret value.

password: ${cmd:pass show squirrel/repo-password}
dsn: ${cmd:op read op://vault/postgres/dsn}

docs.title: ${op://vault/item/field}
docs.text:
1Password native syntax (executed via the op CLI).

password: ${op://Personal/squirrel-repo/password}

docs.title: Named Providers
docs.text: Define reusable provider configurations in the secrets: section, then reference them as ${name:key}.

docs.title: HashiCorp Vault
docs.text:
secrets:
  vault-prod:
    type: vault
    address: https://vault.example.com
    token-from: env:VAULT_TOKEN    # or file:/path/to/token

Reference:

password: ${vault-prod:secret/data/squirrel#repo_password}
dsn: ${vault-prod:secret/data/postgres#replication_dsn}

Path format: <mount>/<path>#<field> (KVv2)

docs.title: Vault Fields
docs.text: Field Description type vault address Vault server URL token-from Token source: env:VAR or file:/path

docs.title: Mozilla SOPS
docs.text:
secrets:
  sops-secrets:
    type: sops
    file: /etc/squirrel/secrets.enc.yaml

Reference:

password: ${sops-secrets:repos/s3-prod/password}
dsn: ${sops-secrets:mysql/prod/dsn}

Path format: dot-separated key path into the decrypted YAML structure.

docs.title: SOPS Fields
docs.text:
Field Description type sops file Path to the SOPS-encrypted YAML file

Requires sops binary on PATH and appropriate key access (GPG, AWS KMS, age, etc.).

docs.title: OS Keyring
docs.text:
secrets:
  keyring-prod:
    type: keyring
    service: squirrel-prod

Reference:

password: ${keyring-prod:repo-password}

docs.title: Keyring Fields
docs.text: Field Description type keyring service Keyring service namespace

docs.title: age
docs.text:
secrets:
  age-key:
    type: age
    file: /etc/squirrel/master.age

Reference:

password: ${age-key:}

The entire decrypted file contents are used as the secret value. The path after : is ignored for age.

docs.title: age Fields
docs.text:
Field Description type age file Path to the age-encrypted file

Requires age binary on PATH and AGE_SECRET_KEY environment variable (or SSH key).

docs.title: Plain File (named)
docs.text:
secrets:
  file-key:
    type: file
    file: /etc/squirrel/repo.key

Reference:

password: ${file-key:}

Equivalent to the inline ${file:/etc/squirrel/repo.key} syntax but reusable by name.

docs.title: File Fields
docs.text: Field Description type file file Path to the plaintext key file

docs.title: Secret Provider Comparison
docs.text: Provider Type Use Case env: Built-in CI/CD, Docker secrets via env vars file: Built-in Key files, Kubernetes secrets mounted as files keyring: Built-in / Named Interactive workstations, macOS/Windows desktops cmd: Built-in 1Password, pass, custom scripts op:// Built-in 1Password native syntax vault Named Production servers with HashiCorp Vault sops Named GitOps with encrypted YAML committed to source age Named Simple age-encrypted secret files file Named Reusable file references across multiple fields

docs.title: Combining Providers
docs.text:
Multiple providers can be used within the same config:

secrets:
  vault-prod:
    type: vault
    address: https://vault.example.com
    token-from: env:VAULT_TOKEN
  sops-secrets:
    type: sops
    file: /etc/squirrel/secrets.enc.yaml

repositories:
  s3-prod:
    url: s3:my-bucket/prod
    password: ${vault-prod:secret/data/squirrel#password}
    env:
      AWS_ACCESS_KEY_ID: ${sops-secrets:aws/access_key_id}
      AWS_SECRET_ACCESS_KEY: ${sops-secrets:aws/secret_access_key}

docs.title: MySQL / MariaDB
docs.text: squirrel supports logical (SQL dump), physical (data directory), and binlog-based backups for MySQL and MariaDB.

docs.title: Supported Versions
docs.text:
Server Minimum Version MySQL 5.7, 8.0, 8.4 MariaDB 10.5, 10.6, 11.x

squirrel automatically detects the server flavor (MySQL vs MariaDB) and adapts accordingly.

docs.title: Binary Logging
docs.text:
Binary logging must be enabled in my.cnf:

[mysqld]
log_bin = ON           # or log_bin = /var/log/mysql/mysql-bin
binlog_format = ROW    # recommended for consistent PITR

docs.title: User Privileges
docs.text:
CREATE USER 'squirrel'@'%' IDENTIFIED BY 'secret';
GRANT RELOAD, REPLICATION SLAVE, REPLICATION CLIENT, SELECT ON *.* TO 'squirrel'@'%';
FLUSH PRIVILEGES;

docs.title: For GTID Support
docs.text:
[mysqld]
gtid_mode = ON
enforce_gtid_consistency = ON

docs.title: Logical Dump (default)
docs.text:
A consistent SQL dump using FLUSH TABLES WITH READ LOCK + START TRANSACTION WITH CONSISTENT SNAPSHOT.

squirrel backup mysql \
  --dsn "root:secret@tcp(localhost:3306)/" \
  --repo /backup/mysql-repo \
  --database myapp \
  --database auditdb \
  --tag daily

The lock is held only briefly to record the binlog position, then released. The dump proceeds from the consistent snapshot without holding the lock.

Creates: mysql-dump snapshot with binlog_file, binlog_pos, and gtid_set (if GTID is enabled).

docs.title: Binlog Streaming
docs.text:
Stream binlog events from the server using MySQL replication protocol, starting from the position recorded in the latest dump snapshot.

squirrel backup mysql \
  --dsn "root:secret@tcp(localhost:3306)/" \
  --repo /backup/mysql-repo \
  --binlog-only

Press Ctrl-C to stop. Run continuously for near-zero RPO.

Creates: mysql-binlog snapshots (one per 16 MiB of binlog data).

docs.title: Physical Backup
docs.text:
Copy the MySQL data directory while holding FLUSH TABLES WITH READ LOCK.

squirrel backup mysql \
  --dsn "root:secret@tcp(localhost:3306)/" \
  --repo /backup/mysql-repo \
  --physical \
  --data-dir /var/lib/mysql

Warning

Physical backup holds the global read lock for the duration of the copy. Plan for downtime or use a replica.

Creates: mysql-physical snapshot.

docs.title: Config Profile
docs.text:
profiles:
  mysql-prod:
    extends: _base-retention
    repository: s3-prod
    type: mysql
    dsn: ${sops-secrets:mysql/prod/dsn}
    databases:
      - appdb
      - auditdb
    tags: [mysql, production]
    schedule: "0 1 * * *"
    hooks:
      post-success:
        - webhook: https://monitoring.example.com/healthcheck/mysql-prod
      post-failure:
        - webhook: https://alerts.example.com/mysql-prod-backup-failed

  mysql-dev:
    repository: local
    type: mysql
    dsn: root:rootpw@tcp(localhost:3306)/
    databases:
      - devdb
    tags: [mysql, dev]
    retention:
      keep-last: 5
      prune: true

docs.title: DSN Format
docs.text:
user:password@tcp(host:port)/
user:password@tcp(host:port)/dbname
root:secret@tcp(localhost:3306)/
squirrel:pw@tcp(db.example.com:3306)/

docs.title: SQL Restore Only
docs.text:
Restore the SQL dump without extracting binlogs:

squirrel restore mysql abc12345 \
  --repo /backup/mysql-repo \
  --dsn "root:secret@tcp(localhost:3306)/" \
  --sql-only

docs.title: SQL Restore + Binlog Extraction for PITR
docs.text:
squirrel restore mysql abc12345 \
  --repo /backup/mysql-repo \
  --dsn "root:secret@tcp(localhost:3306)/" \
  --binlog-dir /tmp/binlogs

Then replay binlogs:

# Replay all binlogs
mysqlbinlog /tmp/binlogs/*.bin | mysql -uroot -p

# PITR to specific time
mysqlbinlog --stop-datetime="2026-04-20 14:30:00" \
  /tmp/binlogs/*.bin | mysql -uroot -p

# PITR with GTID
mysqlbinlog --include-gtids="abc123:1-1000" \
  /tmp/binlogs/*.bin | mysql -uroot -p

docs.title: Physical Restore
docs.text:
squirrel restore mysql abc12345 \
  --repo /backup/mysql-repo \
  --target /var/lib/mysql \
  --innodb-recovery 1

After restore:

chown -R mysql:mysql /var/lib/mysql
systemctl start mysql

docs.title: Snapshot Types
docs.text:
Type Contents Key Metadata mysql-dump SQL dump blobs binlog_file, binlog_pos, gtid_set mysql-binlog Binlog event files segments[], dump_snapshot mysql-physical Data directory files binlog_file, binlog_pos, gtid_set, data_dir

List dump snapshots:

squirrel snapshots --repo /backup/mysql-repo --type mysql-dump

List binlog archives:

squirrel snapshots --repo /backup/mysql-repo --type mysql-binlog

docs.title: GTID vs Position-Based Replication
docs.text:
Feature Position-based GTID Config Default Requires gtid_mode = ON Stored in snapshot binlog_file + binlog_pos gtid_set Replay flexibility Tied to specific file+position Position-independent MariaDB GTID Uses domain_id:server_id:seq Different format from MySQL

squirrel stores both binlog_pos and gtid_set (when available) in the snapshot metadata.

docs.title: Best Practices
docs.text:
1. Use GTID mode for simpler and more reliable PITR.
  2. Run binlog streaming continuously to minimize RPO.
  3. Back up all databases by omitting --database unless you need selective backup.
  4. Test restores regularly in a staging environment.
  5. Use binlog_format = ROW for the most consistent and replayable binlog events.
  6. Use a read replica for physical backups to avoid locking the primary.

docs.title: PostgreSQL
docs.text: squirrel provides physical backup and point-in-time recovery for PostgreSQL using the native replication protocol — no pg_dump, no external tools required.

docs.title: How It Works
docs.text:
sequenceDiagram
    autonumber
    participant SQ as squirrel
    participant PG as PostgreSQL

    SQ->>PG: replication connection (BASE_BACKUP protocol)
    PG-->>SQ: base.tar (PGDATA)
    PG-->>SQ: pg_tblspc_OID.tar (tablespaces)
    SQ->>SQ: store as blobs → create postgres-base snapshot
    SQ->>PG: WAL streaming from start_lsn
    loop Every 16 MiB WAL segment
        PG-->>SQ: WAL segment
        SQ->>SQ: store as blobs → create postgres-wal snapshot
    end

squirrel uses the same protocol as pg_basebackup and streaming replication slaves. No superuser is required — only REPLICATION privilege.

docs.title: PostgreSQL Version
docs.text: PostgreSQL 12 or later. Both the V1 (<= PG 14) and V2 (>= PG 15) base backup streaming protocols are supported automatically.

docs.title: PostgreSQL Configuration
docs.text:
# postgresql.conf
wal_level = replica          # or logical
max_wal_senders = 5          # at least 2
max_replication_slots = 5    # at least 1

docs.title: Replication User
docs.text:
CREATE USER squirrel REPLICATION LOGIN PASSWORD 'secret';

-- Allow reading all databases for the dump connection (PG 14+)
GRANT pg_read_all_data TO squirrel;

docs.title: pg_hba.conf
docs.text:
Allow the squirrel host to connect for replication:

# TYPE  DATABASE     USER       ADDRESS         METHOD
host    replication  squirrel   10.0.0.0/8     scram-sha-256

For local Docker testing (any host, no TLS):

host    replication  all        0.0.0.0/0       trust

docs.title: Full Backup (base + WAL)
docs.text:
squirrel backup postgres \
  --dsn "postgres://squirrel:secret@localhost/postgres?sslmode=disable" \
  --repo /backup/pg-repo \
  --slot squirrel \
  --tag production

This command: 1. Performs a base backup (physical copy of PGDATA). 2. Starts WAL streaming from the base backup's start LSN. 3. Runs until Ctrl-C is pressed or the context is cancelled.

docs.title: WAL-Only Streaming
docs.text:
squirrel backup postgres \
  --dsn "postgres://squirrel:secret@localhost/postgres?sslmode=disable" \
  --repo /backup/pg-repo \
  --slot squirrel \
  --wal-only

Start a dedicated WAL archiver after an initial base backup. Use a scheduled job or squirrel daemon to run this continuously.

docs.title: Config Profile
docs.text:
profiles:
  pg-prod:
    repository: s3-prod
    type: postgres
    dsn: ${vault-prod:secret/data/postgres#replication_url}
    slot: squirrel
    tags: [postgres, production]
    schedule: "0 3 * * *"
    retention:
      keep-last: 5
      keep-daily: 14
      keep-weekly: 8
      prune: true
    hooks:
      pre-backup:
        - command: [psql, -c, "CHECKPOINT"]
      post-success:
        - webhook: https://monitoring.example.com/healthcheck/pg-prod
      post-failure:
        - webhook: https://alerts.example.com/pg-prod-backup-failed

docs.title: What is WAL?
docs.text:
PostgreSQL's Write-Ahead Log (WAL) records every change to the database in a sequential log. WAL segments are fixed-size files (16 MiB by default) written to pg_wal/.

squirrel streams WAL segments via the physical replication protocol and stores them as postgres-wal snapshots. This enables:

  • Point-in-Time Recovery (PITR) — restore the database to any moment between the base backup and the last WAL segment.
  • Near-zero RPO — WAL is archived continuously, not just at backup time.

docs.title: WAL Segment Names
docs.text:
WAL segments are named <timeline><lsn_high><lsn_low>, e.g.:

000000010000000000000005

squirrel stores segments with their original names for compatibility with restore_command.

docs.title: Basic Restore (recover to latest)
docs.text:
squirrel restore postgres abc12345 \
  --repo /backup/pg-repo \
  --target /var/lib/postgresql/17/main \
  --verify

docs.title: PITR — Restore to Specific Time
docs.text:
squirrel restore postgres abc12345 \
  --repo /backup/pg-repo \
  --target /var/lib/postgresql/17/main \
  --pitr "2026-04-20 14:30:00"

docs.title: PITR — Restore to Specific LSN
docs.text:
squirrel restore postgres abc12345 \
  --repo /backup/pg-repo \
  --target /var/lib/postgresql/17/main \
  --pitr-lsn "0/5000028"

docs.title: What Restore Does
docs.text:
1. Extracts base.tar to --target (the PostgreSQL data directory).
  2. Rewrites tablespace symlinks to local paths.
  3. Extracts all WAL segments from matching postgres-wal snapshots to --wal-dir.
  4. Writes recovery.signal to the data directory.
  5. Appends recovery configuration to postgresql.auto.conf: 
    restore_command = 'cp /tmp/wal-dir/%f %p'
recovery_target_time = '2026-04-20 14:30:00'   # if --pitr
recovery_target_action = 'promote'

docs.title: Starting PostgreSQL After Restore
docs.text:
chown -R postgres:postgres /var/lib/postgresql/17/main
systemctl start postgresql@17-main
tail -f /var/log/postgresql/postgresql-17-main.log

PostgreSQL detects recovery.signal, applies WAL, stops at the target, promotes to primary.

docs.title: Why Slots Matter
docs.text: A physical replication slot prevents PostgreSQL from discarding WAL segments that squirrel has not yet archived. Without a slot, the primary may recycle WAL before squirrel reads it.

docs.title: Slot Retention Risk
docs.text:
If squirrel stops streaming WAL for an extended period, the slot causes the primary to accumulate WAL indefinitely. Monitor slot lag:

SELECT slot_name, restart_lsn, pg_size_pretty(
  pg_wal_lsn_diff(pg_current_wal_lsn(), restart_lsn)
) AS lag
FROM pg_replication_slots;

docs.title: Drop a Stuck Slot
docs.text:
squirrel pg drop-slot \
  --dsn "postgres://squirrel:secret@localhost/postgres?sslmode=disable" \
  --slot squirrel

docs.title: Snapshot Types
docs.text:
Type Contents Key Metadata postgres-base Physical PGDATA (base.tar + tablespace TARs) start_lsn, system_id, timeline postgres-wal WAL segment files slot, timeline, segments[], base_snapshot, system_id

List base backups:

squirrel snapshots --repo /backup/pg-repo --type postgres-base

List WAL archives:

squirrel snapshots --repo /backup/pg-repo --type postgres-wal

docs.title: Best Practices
docs.text:
1. Run WAL streaming continuously alongside scheduled base backups to minimize RPO.
  2. Monitor slot lag — alert if WAL accumulation exceeds a safe threshold (e.g. 10 GiB).
  3. Verify restores regularly by testing recovery in a staging environment.
  4. Use --verify on every restore to confirm WAL coverage.
  5. Set a retention policy that keeps enough WAL for your recovery window: 
    retention:
  keep-last: 5        # base backups
  keep-daily: 14
  prune: true
  6. Run CHECKPOINT in a pre-backup hook to minimize recovery time after restore.

docs.title: Monitoring
docs.text:
squirrel exposes Prometheus metrics when running in daemon mode. Use these to track backup health, durations, and errors.

flowchart LR
    SQ[squirrel daemon\n--metrics :9090] -- scrape --> PROM[Prometheus]
    PROM --> GRAFANA[Grafana\ndashboards]
    PROM --> ALERT[Alertmanager\nalerts]
    SQ -- webhook --> HC[healthchecks.io\nUptime Kuma]

docs.title: Enabling Metrics
docs.text:
Pass --metrics <addr> to squirrel daemon:

squirrel daemon --metrics :9090

Metrics are available at:

http://host:9090/metrics

docs.title: Exposed Metrics
docs.text:
Metric Type Description squirrel_backup_duration_seconds Histogram Backup job duration in seconds, labeled by profile squirrel_backup_bytes_processed Counter Total bytes processed across all backups squirrel_backup_errors_total Counter Total number of backup errors, labeled by profile squirrel_last_success_timestamp Gauge Unix timestamp of the last successful backup, labeled by profile

Standard Go runtime metrics (go_goroutines, go_memstats_*, etc.) are also exposed.

docs.title: Prometheus Configuration
docs.text:
# prometheus.yml
scrape_configs:
  - job_name: squirrel
    static_configs:
      - targets: ['squirrel-host:9090']
    scrape_interval: 60s

docs.title: Backup Not Running
docs.text:
groups:
  - name: squirrel
    rules:
      - alert: SquirrelBackupNotRunning
        expr: time() - squirrel_last_success_timestamp{profile="pg-prod"} > 86400
        for: 1h
        labels:
          severity: warning
        annotations:
          summary: "squirrel backup {{ $labels.profile }} has not succeeded in 24h"

docs.title: Backup Errors
docs.text:
- alert: SquirrelBackupErrors
        expr: increase(squirrel_backup_errors_total[1h]) > 0
        labels:
          severity: critical
        annotations:
          summary: "squirrel backup errors detected for {{ $labels.profile }}"

docs.title: Grafana Dashboard
docs.text:
Import a dashboard using the following panel queries:

Backup duration (last 7 days): 

rate(squirrel_backup_duration_seconds_sum[24h])
  / rate(squirrel_backup_duration_seconds_count[24h])

Time since last success per profile: 

time() - squirrel_last_success_timestamp

Error rate: 

rate(squirrel_backup_errors_total[1h])

docs.title: Healthcheck Webhooks
docs.text:
As an alternative to Prometheus, use healthcheck webhooks in hooks to integrate with services like healthchecks.io or Uptime Kuma:

hooks:
  post-success:
    - webhook: https://hc-ping.com/your-uuid-here
  post-failure:
    - webhook: https://hc-ping.com/your-uuid-here/fail

healthchecks.io will alert you if the ping is not received within the expected window.

docs.title: Log Output
docs.text:
squirrel uses structured logging (log/slog) with the following key fields:

Field Description level INFO, WARN, ERROR, DEBUG msg Log message profile Profile name (in daemon mode) blobs Blob count bytes Byte count startLSN PostgreSQL WAL position segments WAL/binlog segment count

Example log output:

2026-04-25 02:00:01 INFO backup started profile=pg-prod
2026-04-25 02:00:03 INFO base backup started startLSN=0/5000000 tablespaces=0
2026-04-25 02:00:45 INFO tablespace streamed label=base bytes=2147483648 blobs=512
2026-04-25 02:00:46 INFO base backup finished endLSN=0/6000000
2026-04-25 02:00:46 INFO WAL streaming started slot=squirrel startLSN=0/5000000
2026-04-25 02:00:56 INFO WAL segment flushed name=000000010000000000000005 bytes=16777216

docs.title: Scheduling
docs.text:
squirrel provides two approaches to automated scheduling: a built-in daemon scheduler and OS-native scheduler integration.

flowchart LR
    subgraph Daemon["squirrel daemon (containers / VMs)"]
        CRON[built-in cron\nscheduler] --> RUN[squirrel run\nprofile]
        RUN --> METRICS[Prometheus\nmetrics :9090]
    end
    subgraph OS["OS-native scheduler"]
        SYSTEMD[systemd timer] & LAUNCHD[launchd plist] & TASKS[Task Scheduler] --> RUN2[squirrel run\nprofile]
    end
    CONFIG[config.yml\nschedule: cron] --> Daemon & OS

docs.title: Option 1: squirrel daemon (recommended for containers)
docs.text:
squirrel daemon runs as a foreground process with a built-in cron scheduler. It reads schedule: fields from all profiles and fires them at the configured times.

squirrel daemon --metrics :9090

docs.title: Advantages
docs.text:
• Single process, no external scheduler required.
  • Works inside Docker/Kubernetes without systemd.
  • Live config reload via SIGHUP.
  • Built-in Prometheus metrics.

docs.title: Docker Compose
docs.text:
services:
  squirrel:
    image: ghcr.io/elpol4k0/squirrel:latest
    command: daemon --metrics :9090
    environment:
      SQUIRREL_PASSWORD: ${SQUIRREL_PASSWORD}
    volumes:
      - ./config.yml:/root/.config/squirrel/config.yml:ro
    restart: unless-stopped

docs.title: Kubernetes
docs.text:
apiVersion: apps/v1
kind: Deployment
metadata:
  name: squirrel
spec:
  replicas: 1
  template:
    spec:
      containers:
        - name: squirrel
          image: ghcr.io/elpol4k0/squirrel:latest
          args: ["daemon", "--metrics", ":9090"]
          env:
            - name: SQUIRREL_PASSWORD
              valueFrom:
                secretKeyRef:
                  name: squirrel-secret
                  key: password
          volumeMounts:
            - name: config
              mountPath: /root/.config/squirrel

docs.title: Option 2: OS-Native Scheduler
docs.text: Install OS-level tasks for individual profiles using squirrel schedule install.

docs.title: Linux (systemd)
docs.text:
# Install (reads schedule: from profile config)
squirrel schedule install pg-daily

# Enable and start the timer
systemctl --user enable --now squirrel-pg-daily.timer

# Check status
systemctl --user status squirrel-pg-daily.timer

# View logs
journalctl --user -u squirrel-pg-daily.service

Generated files: 

~/.config/systemd/user/squirrel-pg-daily.service
~/.config/systemd/user/squirrel-pg-daily.timer

For system-wide installation (run as root): 

sudo squirrel schedule install pg-daily --config /etc/squirrel/config.yml
sudo systemctl enable --now squirrel-pg-daily.timer

docs.title: macOS (launchd)
docs.text:
squirrel schedule install pg-daily

# Load the plist
launchctl load ~/Library/LaunchAgents/io.squirrel.pg-daily.plist

# Check status
launchctl print gui/$(id -u)/io.squirrel.pg-daily

Generated file: 

~/Library/LaunchAgents/io.squirrel.pg-daily.plist

docs.title: Windows (Task Scheduler)
docs.text:
squirrel schedule install pg-daily

# Verify in Task Scheduler
Get-ScheduledTask -TaskName "squirrel-pg-daily"

# Run immediately for testing
Start-ScheduledTask -TaskName "squirrel-pg-daily"

Created entry: Task Scheduler → \squirrel-pg-daily

docs.title: Cron Expression Reference
docs.text:
squirrel uses standard 5-field cron syntax:

┌───── minute    (0–59)
│ ┌──── hour      (0–23)
│ │ ┌─── day       (1–31)
│ │ │ ┌── month     (1–12)
│ │ │ │ ┌─ weekday   (0–7, 0 and 7 = Sunday)
│ │ │ │ │
* * * * *

docs.title: Common Schedules
docs.text: Expression When 0 2 * * * Daily at 02:00 30 1 * * * Daily at 01:30 0 3 * * 0 Weekly on Sunday at 03:00 0 */6 * * * Every 6 hours 0 0 1 * * Monthly on the 1st at midnight */15 * * * * Every 15 minutes @daily Daily at midnight @weekly Weekly on Sunday at midnight @monthly Monthly on the 1st at midnight @hourly Every hour

docs.title: In Config Profile
docs.text:
profiles:
  pg-prod:
    repository: s3-prod
    type: postgres
    dsn: ${env:PG_DSN}
    slot: squirrel
    schedule: "0 3 * * *"     # Daily at 03:00

  pg-prod-offsite:
    extends: pg-prod
    repository: sftp-offsite
    schedule: "0 5 * * 0"     # Weekly on Sunday at 05:00

  files-home:
    repository: local
    paths: [/home/user]
    schedule: "@daily"

docs.title: Managing Schedules
docs.text:
# List installed schedules
squirrel schedule list

# Remove a schedule
squirrel schedule remove pg-daily

# Remove all squirrel schedules
squirrel schedule list | xargs squirrel schedule remove
